proposals.title: Multi-Flex: High-Throughput LLM Inference by Orchestrating Heterogeneous, Low-End GPU Clusters
proposals.motivation: Prior work like FlexGen has pushed single-GPU, CPU/disk offloading to its limits, but remains fundamentally bottlenecked by the memory and compute of one device. This excludes users with access only to clusters of older or low-end GPUs (e.g., multiple GTX 1080 Ti or T4 GPUs) from efficient large model inference. A system that can cohesively leverage multiple such devices as a unified, high-throughput inference platform would democratize access and improve resource utilization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by dynamically partitioning and scheduling the computational graph (layers) and key-value cache (tokens) across multiple low-end GPUs with optimized inter-device communication, we can achieve higher aggregate throughput and lower latency for large language model inference than a single-GPU offloading system with equivalent total hardware cost.
proposals.method: We will design a system that extends the FlexGen scheduler to manage multiple GPUs. The method involves: 1) A profiling stage to characterize inter-GPU bandwidth and individual GPU memory/compute, 2) A dynamic, layer-wise partitioning algorithm that assigns model blocks to GPUs, balancing load and minimizing communication, informed by Chen's work on InRank for low-rank adaptations across partitions, and 3) A distributed, paged attention mechanism that manages the KV cache across GPU memories, fetching token states on-demand with minimal synchronization overhead.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel open-source system for multi-GPU, memory-constrained inference, expanding the design space beyond single-device offloading. It will provide a formal model and empirical evaluation of the trade-offs between inter-GPU communication, layer parallelism, and KV cache management for throughput optimization on heterogeneous low-end clusters.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing multi-GPU strategies designed for high-end, homogeneous hardware with fast interconnects, this system is specifically designed for the heterogeneity and bandwidth constraints of low-end GPU clusters, treating them as a pooled, tiered memory-compute hierarchy rather than a symmetric parallel computer.

proposals.title: FlexGen-Arch: High-Throughput Inference for Non-Transformer Architectures via Dynamic Memory Offloading
proposals.motivation: The rapid scaling of generative AI has been dominated by Transformer models, leading to a research focus on optimizing their inference (e.g., FlexGen). However, efficient alternative architectures like State Space Models (e.g., Mamba), RWKV, and Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models exhibit fundamentally different memory access patterns and computational graphs. To ensure a diverse and efficient future AI ecosystem, principled high-throughput inference methods for these non-Transformer models are critically needed, especially for resource-constrained deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: The core principles of high-throughput offloading and scheduling developed for Transformers can be generalized and adapted to create efficient, single-GPU inference systems for state-space and recurrent architectures, by explicitly modeling and optimizing for their unique sequential dependency and memory patterns.
proposals.method: We will extend the FlexGen framework to support non-Transformer backbones. First, we will profile the memory footprint (activation, KV cache for hybrid models, state tensors) and computational trace of target architectures like Mamba and RWKV. Second, we will design a new dynamic offloading scheduler that accounts for their sequential, non-attention dependencies, potentially treating recurrent states as high-priority pinned memory. Third, we will integrate incremental low-rank learning (InRank) principles to compress any remaining linear layers, further reducing the working set size for offloading.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first open-source, high-throughput inference system optimized for modern non-Transformer architectures, enabling their practical deployment on a single GPU. It will provide a systematic analysis of memory bottlenecks in these models and establish a generalized methodology for architecture-aware offloading.
proposals.novelty: While offloading for Transformers is well-studied, applying these principles to models with strong sequential dependencies and no standard attention KV cache is a novel challenge. The key innovation is an offloading scheduler whose policy is derived from the dataflow graph of state-passing architectures, not just layer-wise execution.

proposals.title: FlexGen-LS: A Low-Latency Offloading Engine for Real-Time LLM Inference on a Single GPU
proposals.motivation: The FlexGen framework demonstrated high-throughput LLM inference via offloading, but it is optimized for batch processing, incurring high latency unsuitable for interactive applications like chatbots or code assistants. As of 2025, deploying large models for latency-sensitive tasks on resource-constrained devices remains a critical challenge, requiring a fundamental redesign of offloading strategies to prioritize time-to-first-token over total batch throughput.
proposals.hypothesis: By redesigning FlexGen's scheduling policy and memory management to minimize the critical path of token generation, we can achieve sub-second latency for large-model inference on a single GPU without sacrificing model quality.
proposals.method: We will develop a latency-optimized scheduler that prioritizes the preloading of critical attention layers and KV cache blocks into GPU memory, informed by a real-time profile of the generation process. This involves integrating an incremental, low-rank (InRank-style) adapter to compress intermediate activations for faster CPU-GPU transfer. We will implement a token-level speculative execution engine that overlaps computation of non-critical layers with I/O operations, and rigorously evaluate the trade-off between latency and throughput across different model sizes and sequence lengths.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a publicly available, open-source system that extends the applicability of offloading-based inference to real-time, interactive use cases. It will provide a formal analysis of the latency-throughput trade-off in offloading systems and establish new benchmarks for single-GPU, low-latency LLM serving.
proposals.novelty: While prior work like FlexGen and LLM.inference focused on maximizing throughput, this proposal explicitly re-architects the offloading paradigm for low-latency, a non-trivial shift requiring new scheduling and prefetching algorithms. The integration of incremental low-rank techniques for activation compression to reduce I/O bottlenecks is a novel combination for latency reduction.

proposals.title: DynamicCache: Adaptive Attention Cache Compression for Efficient Long-Context Inference
proposals.motivation: Deploying large language models for long-context tasks is bottlenecked by the memory footprint of the attention key-value cache, which grows linearly with sequence length. Prior static compression methods (e.g., uniform sparsification, fixed-rank projection) fail to adapt to the varying importance of tokens across different layers and tasks, leading to unnecessary accuracy loss or suboptimal memory savings. An adaptive, context-aware compression strategy is needed to maximize throughput and enable longer contexts on memory-constrained hardware like a single GPU.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the compressibility of the attention cache varies predictably based on layer depth, token position, and task semantics, and that a lightweight online policy can dynamically adjust compression parameters to preserve critical information while aggressively reducing memory.
proposals.method: We will develop DynamicCache, which integrates a lightweight, learned gating network that analyzes a small window of recent activations and cache statistics to predict per-layer, per-head compression parameters (e.g., sparsity ratio for token eviction, rank for low-rank projection). This policy network will be trained via reinforcement learning, using a reward function balancing memory reduction against a downstream task performance proxy (e.g., next-token perplexity). We will build upon and extend the low-rank learning techniques from InRank and the memory-aware scheduling from FlexGen.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical system for dynamic KV cache management that outperforms static baselines in memory-accuracy trade-offs across diverse long-context tasks. It will contribute a novel framework for online, adaptive model compression specifically tailored for the inference-time attention mechanism, advancing the field of high-throughput generative inference.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work applying fixed compression, DynamicCache introduces a context- and task-conditioned policy for real-time adaptation, and it jointly optimizes heterogeneous compression strategies (sparsity and low-rank) across different layers and attention heads.

proposals.title: Throughput-Constrained Model Scaling: An Inverse Optimization Framework for Single-GPU Large Model Inference
proposals.motivation: The deployment of large language models is bottlenecked by GPU memory and compute, forcing a trade-off between model size and inference speed. Existing work like FlexGen optimizes throughput for a fixed model, but practitioners often have a fixed latency/throughput target and seek the largest model meeting it. This inverse problem is critical for cost-effective deployment but lacks a systematic solution.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by co-optimizing over model architecture parameters (layer count, hidden size, attention heads) and inference-time techniques (activation checkpointing, selective attention, low-rank adaptations), we can algorithmically find the Pareto-optimal model size for any given hardware and throughput constraint.
proposals.method: We will develop an inverse optimization framework building on Chen's prior work in InRank and high-throughput inference. The method uses a differentiable proxy model of inference latency, incorporating memory-bandwidth and compute bottlenecks for key operations (attention, feed-forward). Starting from a baseline model, we will perform constrained gradient-based search over a continuous relaxation of architectural dimensions, coupled with discrete decisions on techniques like KV cache compression and FlexGen-like offloading. The search will be guided by incremental low-rank learning to efficiently evaluate candidate configurations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical tool and set of principles for deploying the largest possible capable model under strict service-level agreements. It will yield a novel benchmarking suite comparing optimal models across hardware targets and contribute to theory on the joint scaling of architecture and inference algorithms.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior model scaling laws which focus on training loss, or inference systems which fix the model, we formally invert the throughput constraint to solve for maximal model size, creating a new co-design paradigm between architecture and runtime.

proposals.title: FlexGen-MM: High-Throughput Multi-Modal Inference via Unified Layer- and Token-Level Offloading
proposals.motivation: The rise of large multi-modal models (LMMs) like Flamingo and GPT-4V creates a critical inference bottleneck, as their massive parameter counts and heterogeneous data streams (images, audio, text) exceed single-GPU memory. Pre-2026 high-throughput offloading methods like FlexGen are designed for text-only LLMs and do not address the unique computational patterns and memory hierarchies of multi-modal fusion architectures, limiting their accessibility.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a unified offloading scheduler, co-designed for the distinct computational graphs and activation patterns of vision-language-audio models, can achieve high-throughput multi-modal inference on a single GPU by dynamically prioritizing the movement of layers and tokens across CPU and GPU memory.
proposals.method: We will extend the FlexGen framework to support models with modality-specific encoders (e.g., ViT, audio spectrogram transformers) and a fusion transformer. The core is a **Unified Multi-Modal Scheduler** that profiles the memory footprint and latency of processing image patches, audio frames, and text tokens. It will implement **heterogeneous chunking**, where offloading granularity (layer vs. sub-layer) and prefetching strategy are adapted per modality based on token sequence length and activation size. We will integrate **InRank-inspired low-rank adaptations** for encoder outputs to reduce intermediate activation memory before fusion, complementing the offloading.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first open-source, high-throughput offloading system optimized for large multi-modal models, enabling their execution on commodity hardware. It will provide a systematic analysis of memory-compute trade-offs in multi-modal inference and establish new benchmarks for throughput on single-GPU systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving beyond text-only offloading to a co-designed scheduler for multi-modal computational graphs, dynamically managing heterogeneous data streams (image patches, audio frames, text tokens) which have fundamentally different memory-access patterns compared to homogeneous text tokens.

proposals.title: Beyond Autoregression: Analyzing Training Dynamics and Emergent Representations Under Non-Predictive Losses in Transformers
proposals.motivation: The transformer architecture has achieved dominance in sequence modeling almost exclusively through next-token prediction (NTP) loss. However, NTP is a specific, sequential objective that may not be optimal for all tasks, such as retrieval, planning, or latent representation learning. Understanding how transformers behave under alternative loss functions is crucial for developing more efficient, controllable, and capable models for diverse applications beyond pure generation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training transformers with non-predictive losses (e.g., contrastive, masked modeling, or ranking losses) induces fundamentally different training dynamics and leads to the emergence of distinct, potentially more structured, internal representations compared to standard autoregressive training.
proposals.method: We will conduct a systematic empirical study using medium-scale transformer architectures. We will replace the standard causal language modeling head with task-specific heads for losses including SimCSE-style contrastive loss, BERT-style masked token modeling, and a ranking loss based on document relevance. Leveraging Beidi Chen's expertise in low-rank learning (InRank) and efficient training/inference (FlexGen), we will instrument the models to analyze dynamics: tracking layer-wise representation evolution via Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA), measuring attention pattern shifts, and evaluating the rank and memory footprint of intermediate activations throughout training.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive analysis of transformer training dynamics under a spectrum of non-predictive losses. It will yield practical insights for designing more efficient training recipes for non-autoregressive tasks and may reveal theoretical connections between loss functions, optimization geometry, and representational structure.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has applied different losses to transformers, a controlled, mechanistic study comparing their training dynamics and internal representational consequences is lacking. This proposal uniquely combines Chen's focus on efficient training/inference and low-rank analysis to dissect these differences.

proposals.title: InRank-Inverse: Recovering Token Co-occurrence Statistics from Final Attention Weights in Transformer Models
proposals.motivation: Understanding what training data properties are encoded in model weights is a core problem in interpretability and data attribution. Prior to 2026, work like InRank studied low-rank weight updates during training, but the inverse problem—deducing statistical features of the training corpus from the final, fixed attention parameters—remains unexplored. This capability would enable auditing for memorized patterns and validating data provenance without accessing the original dataset.
proposals.hypothesis: The attention weight matrices in a trained Transformer, particularly in later layers, encode a low-rank, factorized representation of the token co-occurrence statistics present in the training data, which can be recovered via constrained optimization.
proposals.method: We will formulate the recovery as a constrained matrix factorization problem. For a given attention head's key (K) and query (Q) projection matrices, we hypothesize their product approximates a transformed co-occurrence matrix. Using the InRank framework to model low-rank structure, we will develop an iterative algorithm that, given W_K and W_Q, solves for a plausible token co-occurrence matrix C subject to constraints (non-negativity, symmetry). We will validate the method on controlled synthetic datasets and small-scale models (e.g., GPT-2) where the true statistics are known, using metrics like Spearman correlation between recovered and true co-occurrence ranks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first algorithmic framework for inferring training data statistics directly from model weights, advancing model interpretability. It will establish a theoretical and empirical link between final attention parameters and corpus-level statistics, offering a new tool for data attribution and model auditing.
proposals.novelty: While existing work analyzes what models learn, the proposed inverse problem of statistically reconstructing training data features from frozen attention weights is a novel direction. It uniquely combines low-rank learning (InRank) with an inference-time analysis to probe training data remnants.

proposals.title: Dual Dynamics: Analyzing Training and Representational Efficiency via Bidirectional Next/Previous Token Prediction
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) are trained almost exclusively on the next-token prediction objective, creating a fundamental asymmetry in how they process sequences. This focus leaves a critical gap in our understanding: how would training dynamics, learned representations, and model efficiency differ if the objective were reversed to predict the previous token? Investigating this symmetric problem can reveal core principles of autoregressive learning and may expose optimization shortcuts or representational biases inherent in the standard approach.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training a model to predict the previous token will lead to distinct, yet complementary, training dynamics and representational structures compared to next-token prediction, and that analyzing these differences will reveal methods to improve training efficiency and model compression.
proposals.method: We will train otherwise-identical transformer architectures from scratch on the same corpus, one with a causal mask for next-token prediction (standard) and one with a reversed causal mask for previous-token prediction (Dual). We will instrument training to compare key dynamics: the evolution of attention patterns (layer- and head-wise), the trajectory of gradient norms, and the intrinsic rank of weight matrices using incremental low-rank analysis (InRank). Inference-time efficiency and memory profiles for both models will be benchmarked using a framework like FlexGen, adapted for the reversed direction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic analysis of the 'dual' training objective for autoregressive transformers, quantifying its differences in dynamics and final representations. We aim to derive insights that could lead to more efficient training curricula or improved low-rank approximations (e.g., for model compression) by leveraging complementary information from the dual training process.
proposals.novelty: While bidirectional context is used in models like BERT, the systematic study of a strictly autoregressive *previous-token* prediction objective—and its direct comparison to standard next-token prediction—is unexplored. The novelty lies in framing this as a symmetric learning problem to dissect fundamental autoregressive training mechanisms.

proposals.title: Scatterbrain for Scalable Attention: A Unified Low-Rank+Random-Feature Framework for Vision and Language Models
proposals.motivation: The quadratic complexity of self-attention remains a critical bottleneck for scaling large vision and language models, especially for long sequences and high-resolution images. While approximations like low-rank or random-feature methods exist, they often trade accuracy for efficiency. Scatterbrain, which unifies low-rank and random-feature attention, has shown promise in T2T-ViT and BigGAN, but its generalizability as a drop-in replacement across diverse architectures and tasks is unexplored, limiting its broader impact.
proposals.hypothesis: Scatterbrain's hybrid approximation can serve as a universally effective, plug-and-play replacement for standard self-attention across a spectrum of pre-trained vision and language models, maintaining high task fidelity while significantly reducing computational and memory costs during fine-tuning and inference.
proposals.method: We will implement Scatterbrain as a drop-in module, replacing standard attention in several large-scale pre-trained models, including Vision Transformers (e.g., ViT, DeiT), language models (e.g., T5, GPT-2 scale), and vision-language models (e.g., CLIP). We will conduct systematic fine-tuning and evaluation on core tasks (image classification, text generation, VQA) using established benchmarks. The study will rigorously compare performance, throughput (inference time), and memory footprint against full attention and other approximations (e.g., Linformer, Performer), leveraging tools like FlexGen for memory-aware profiling.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive empirical analysis of Scatterbrain's versatility, establishing its practical value for efficient model adaptation and deployment. It will deliver a set of validated, efficient model variants and concrete guidelines for practitioners on when and how to apply this unified approximation, advancing the field of high-throughput generative and discriminative inference.
proposals.novelty: While Scatterbrain itself is known, its systematic application as a universal replacement across disparate model families (vision, language, VL) is novel. The investigation bridges Chen's expertise in low-rank learning (InRank) and efficient inference (FlexGen) to test a unified attention approximation at scale, moving beyond initial proof-of-concept demonstrations.

proposals.title: AdaTemp: Adaptive Temperature Scheduling for Optimizing the Sparse vs. Low-Rank Trade-off in Efficient LLM Training and Inference
proposals.motivation: Efficient training and inference of large language models (LLMs) critically depend on approximating the computationally expensive attention mechanism. Pre-2026 methods, such as sparse attention and low-rank approximations (e.g., via InRank), offer distinct trade-offs between accuracy and computational cost. However, these approximations are typically static, failing to adapt to the varying token-level and layer-wise dynamics of the model throughout training or during a generation step, limiting their potential efficiency gains.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dynamically adjusting the softmax temperature in attention heads—effectively controlling the sharpness of the probability distribution—can optimally guide the sparsity or low-rank structure of attention approximations, leading to more computationally efficient training and inference without sacrificing model quality.
proposals.method: We will develop a scheduler that modulates the temperature parameter τ in the softmax function (softmax(QK^T/√d * τ)) during training or per-generation step. This scheduler will be informed by real-time, layer-specific metrics such as attention score entropy, gradient norms, or the singular value spectrum of the attention matrix. We will integrate this with existing sparse (e.g., block-sparse patterns) and low-rank (leveraging InRank's incremental learning) approximation backends. The core algorithm will involve a lightweight controller that adjusts τ to maintain a target approximation error bound, optimizing the memory-time trade-off studied in works like FlexGen.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, adaptive method for controlling approximation fidelity in attention, directly improving the Pareto frontier of the memory-time-accuracy trade-off for LLMs. It will yield a practical, plug-in module for existing efficient training and single-GPU inference pipelines, such as those described in Chen's prior work, enabling higher throughput or lower resource consumption.
proposals.novelty: While temperature scaling is known in model calibration and knowledge distillation, its use as a dynamic, real-time control knob for steering *which* attention approximations (sparse vs. low-rank) are most effective per layer and per token context is novel and non-obvious. This bridges the separate fields of attention mechanism design and efficient systems optimization.

proposals.title: SpectralSparse: Principled Attention Graph Sparsification for Efficient Large Language Model Inference
proposals.motivation: The quadratic complexity of attention in Transformers is a fundamental bottleneck for high-throughput inference, especially for long sequences. Pre-2026 sparse attention heuristics (e.g., BigBird, Longformer) lack a rigorous theoretical foundation for preserving the full-attention operator's spectral properties, potentially degrading model quality. A principled sparsification method could enable more efficient, single-GPU inference for large models without sacrificing accuracy.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying spectral graph sparsification algorithms to the attention score matrix will yield a provably accurate sparse approximation, enabling drastic reductions in memory and compute during inference while maintaining the original model's output fidelity.
proposals.method: We will frame the attention score matrix for a given layer and input as a dense, weighted directed graph. We will adapt spectral sparsification algorithms (e.g., Spielman-Srivastava effective resistance sampling) to sample key-value token pairs, constructing a sparse attention subgraph that approximates the full graph's Laplacian. This sparsified graph will directly define the sparse attention pattern for use in a modified Transformer block. We will integrate this into an inference system like FlexGen, evaluating trade-offs between sparsity, throughput, and task performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a theoretically grounded framework for sparse attention, moving beyond hand-crafted patterns. It will deliver a practical, tunable method for accelerating LLM inference on memory-constrained hardware, validated by empirical results on standard benchmarks. The approach could also inform future low-rank learning techniques like InRank by analyzing the spectral structure of weight matrices.
proposals.novelty: While graph sparsification is well-studied in theoretical CS, its direct application to dynamically constructed attention graphs for LLM acceleration is novel. This bridges a gap between rigorous algorithmic theory and practical deep learning efficiency, offering a principled alternative to existing heuristic or learned sparsity methods.

proposals.title: Decentralized Model-Parallel Training via Incremental Low-Rank Adaptation and Token-Centric Scheduling
proposals.motivation: Current large language model (LLM) training is dominated by synchronous data parallelism, which requires aggregating massive gradients and full model replicas, creating a memory and communication bottleneck. As models exceed single-device memory, hybrid parallelism (e.g., Megatron-LM) introduces complexity. A default model-parallel approach could enable more efficient, decentralized training on heterogeneous, memory-constrained hardware clusters, aligning with trends toward disaggregated compute.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a training system designed with model parallelism as its primary, default parallelism dimension, combined with incremental low-rank updates, can achieve competitive convergence and throughput compared to data-parallel baselines while drastically reducing per-device memory footprint and communication volume.
proposals.method: We will design a decentralized training framework where each GPU holds a unique, contiguous segment of the model layers (e.g., a transformer block). The forward/backward pass flows sequentially through devices, akin to pipeline parallelism, but we eliminate the global sync by using an asynchronous, token-centric scheduling protocol inspired by FlexGen's offloading management. We will integrate InRank for incremental low-rank learning of attention and feed-forward sub-layers to compress the communicated activations and gradients between devices, and employ a novel attention-aware scheduling algorithm to prioritize critical token pathways and minimize pipeline bubbles.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel system design and protocol for decentralized, model-parallel-centric training, 2) empirical analysis of convergence and efficiency when model parallelism is the default, and 3) an open-source framework demonstrating scalable training of multi-billion parameter models on memory-heterogeneous GPU clusters without global batch synchronization.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the standard paradigm to make model parallelism the default and data parallelism optional, and in synthesizing token-centric asynchronous execution with incremental low-rank learning specifically to optimize the communication patterns of this inverted design.

proposals.title: FlexGen-Layer: High-Throughput Inference via Fine-Grained, Dynamic Layer Partitioning
proposals.motivation: As large language models (LLMs) grow, single-GPU inference becomes a severe bottleneck, limiting accessibility. Existing multi-GPU parallelism strategies (e.g., tensor, pipeline) incur significant communication overhead and are inefficient for latency-tolerant, high-throughput scenarios like batch processing. A scheduling-based approach, as pioneered in FlexGen, must be extended beyond coarse-grained attention/value tensor partitioning to more granular levels to maximize memory and compute utilization across heterogeneous devices.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying a unified offloading and scheduling framework to extremely fine-grained, layer-level or sub-layer (e.g., individual linear projections within an attention block) model partitioning will yield superior throughput and memory efficiency compared to existing tensor- or block-level methods, by enabling more dynamic load balancing and overlapping of compute and I/O.
proposals.method: We will extend the FlexGen scheduler to operate on a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of fine-grained computational nodes, where each node corresponds to a sub-layer operation (e.g., Q, K, V projection, MLP layer). The scheduler will dynamically partition this graph across CPU and GPU memory, using pre-2026 optimization techniques like linear programming to minimize the makespan for a batch of sequences. We will integrate this with InRank principles to optionally keep low-rank increments of certain layers resident in GPU memory, further reducing I/O. The system will be evaluated on models like LLaMA-13B/30B under memory constraints of a single high-end GPU with CPU RAM.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel system for LLM inference that democratizes access to larger models by making extreme offloading practical and efficient, 2) a formalization of the fine-grained partitioning and scheduling problem for transformer layers, and 3) open-source code and benchmarks demonstrating order-of-magnitude throughput gains over baseline offloading systems for batched inference.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving beyond partitioning attention/output tensors (FlexGen) or fixed pipeline stages to a dynamic, sub-layer granularity scheduling problem, co-designing it with incremental low-rank learning (InRank) to create a hybrid static-dynamic partitioning strategy unseen in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Decentralized Sparse Training: A Dynamic Scheduler for Efficient Mixture-of-Experts Model Training
proposals.motivation: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models, such as Switch Transformers, offer a path to extreme model scale with manageable computational cost per token. However, their training is memory-intensive and communication-heavy in distributed settings, as experts are sparsely activated and distributed across devices. Pre-2026 decentralized training frameworks like FlexGen focus on inference; a dedicated scheduler for the unique, dynamic load patterns of MoE training is needed to improve throughput and resource utilization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a decentralized training scheduler, which dynamically routes tokens and schedules expert computation based on real-time load and network state, can significantly reduce communication bottlenecks and idle time compared to static allocation, thereby accelerating MoE model training.
proposals.method: We will adapt and extend the principles of high-throughput inference schedulers (like those in FlexGen) for the training loop. The core is a dynamic, token-aware scheduler that monitors expert activation frequencies and GPU memory loads across nodes. It will use incremental low-rank (InRank) techniques to compress and communicate routing metadata efficiently. The scheduler will implement a priority queue for tokens targeting overloaded experts and will prefetch parameters for anticipated expert computations to overlap communication with computation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel open-source scheduler for decentralized MoE training, reducing per-iteration time and memory pressure. It will demonstrate empirically how dynamic scheduling outperforms static baselines on standard MoE benchmarks. The project bridges the gap between efficient inference methods and large-scale sparse model training.
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes MoE inference or uses static parallelism for training, our approach dynamically schedules the decentralized training process itself, treating expert imbalance as a real-time load-balancing problem solvable with token-level granularity and compressed metadata, a direct adaptation of high-throughput inference techniques to the training regime.

proposals.title: UniTrainFuse: Joint Optimization of Training Dynamics and Inference Efficiency via Dynamic Low-Rank Attention
proposals.motivation: The computational lifecycle of large language models is fractured: training algorithms (e.g., full-rank optimization) are designed for loss convergence, while inference systems (e.g., FlexGen, KV caching) are retrofitted for latency and memory, often degrading model quality. This separation creates inefficiency and suboptimal trade-offs. A unified approach is needed to co-design the learning process and its deployment, especially for resource-constrained settings.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating inference-aware constraints—specifically, dynamic token sparsity and low-rank attention structure—directly into the training objective, we can produce models that are simultaneously faster to train and inherently more efficient to infer without sacrificing accuracy.
proposals.method: We will develop UniTrainFuse, a training framework that incorporates a novel Dynamic Low-Rank Attention (DLRA) module. Inspired by InRank, DLRA will incrementally learn and freeze low-rank factorizations of attention heads during training, directly reducing inference-time FLOPs and memory. Concurrently, we will integrate a token efficiency loss, penalizing attention distributions that are computationally expensive to simulate during inference, guided by methods like FlexGen. The training will use a multi-objective loss balancing task accuracy, layer-wise rank reduction, and a token-efficiency regularizer.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel training algorithm that outputs models with built-in inference efficiency, 2) a systematic framework for evaluating the training-inference trade-off frontier, and 3) empirical results demonstrating Pareto-superior models on standard benchmarks, achieving higher throughput per GPU (e.g., a single GPU as in Chen's prior work) than models trained with conventional separation.
proposals.novelty: While low-rank training and efficient inference are studied separately, jointly optimizing for them in a single end-to-end process is unexplored. The key novelty is the direct feedback of inference-time computational graphs (modeled via token and memory constraints) into the gradient-based training dynamics.

proposals.title: LSH-CR: Accelerating CNN and RNN Inference via Locality-Sensitive Hashing of Convolutional Filters and Recurrent States
proposals.motivation: The computational cost of large-scale Convolutional and Recurrent Neural Networks remains prohibitive for real-time or high-throughput inference, especially on resource-constrained devices. While SLIDE demonstrated the power of Locality-Sensitive Hashing (LSH) for sparsifying fully-connected layers, its principles have not been systematically extended to the structured, weight-sharing computations of CNNs and RNNs. This gap presents a significant opportunity to reduce the dominant FLOPs and memory access costs in these foundational architectures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the local activation patterns in CNNs and the temporal evolution of hidden states in RNNs exhibit sufficient structure and locality that they can be efficiently indexed via LSH, enabling selective, approximate computation of only the most relevant operations without significant accuracy loss.
proposals.method: We will adapt the SLIDE algorithm's core LSH-based retrieval mechanism. For CNNs, we will design hash functions that map high-dimensional local input patches and filter weights to the same bucket with high probability when their inner product is large, allowing us to compute only a sparse subset of convolutions per spatial location. For RNNs, we will hash the current hidden state and candidate weight matrices to selectively update only the most relevant state dimensions at each timestep. The system will be integrated into a framework like FlexGen for rigorous evaluation of throughput, memory, and accuracy trade-offs on standard vision and language tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, principled method for sparsifying inference in CNNs and RNNs, extending the benefits of LSH-based acceleration beyond fully-connected layers. It will provide open-source implementations and a detailed analysis of the accuracy-efficiency Pareto frontier, contributing directly to the field of high-throughput inference for non-autoregressive models.
proposals.novelty: While LSH has been used for attention approximation and dense layer sparsification, its application to the core, weight-sharing operations of CNNs and the sequential state updates of RNNs is novel. This moves beyond weight-space sparsity to activation- and state-space sparsity, a non-trivial adaptation requiring new hashing schemes for structured computations.

proposals.title: SLIDE-CRNN: Sub-Linear Memory and Time Training for Convolutional and Recurrent Networks via Locality-Sensitive Hashing
proposals.motivation: The SLIDE (Sub-LInear Deep learning Engine) algorithm demonstrated that locality-sensitive hashing (LSH) could drastically reduce the computational cost of training fully connected layers by approximating top-k attention in the weight-activation product. However, modern architectures heavily rely on convolutional (CNN) and recurrent (RNN) layers, where the dense matrix multiplications differ structurally. Extending SLIDE's principles to these core architectures is crucial for enabling efficient, large-scale training of diverse models on resource-constrained hardware, a key theme in pre-2026 efficient AI research.
proposals.hypothesis: The core computational bottlenecks in CNN and RNN training—specifically, the convolution operations and recurrent state updates—can be reformulated as sparse, similarity-driven problems solvable via LSH-based sampling, leading to sub-linear training time and memory usage without significant accuracy loss.
proposals.method: We will adapt SLIDE's LSH sampling to convolutional filters by hashing local image patches and matching them to a dynamically maintained hash table of filter weights, sparsifying the convolution operation. For RNNs, we will apply LSH to the hidden state and input vector to approximate the dense recurrent matrix multiplication. The project will integrate these modules into a unified framework, SLIDE-CRNN, building on FlexGen's memory management principles for end-to-end efficiency. Evaluation will use standard vision (CIFAR, ImageNet) and sequence (Penn Treebank) benchmarks, comparing accuracy and throughput against full-precision training.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first practical algorithm for sub-linear time training of CNNs and RNNs, extending the benefits of SLIDE beyond fully connected networks. It will provide open-source implementations and rigorous analysis of the trade-offs between sampling efficiency and model accuracy for these architectures.
proposals.novelty: While SLIDE targeted transformer-like attention, applying its LSH-based sampling to the spatially local operations of CNNs and the temporal dependencies of RNNs is a non-trivial reformulation. This bridges the gap between efficient hashing-based algorithms and the dominant pre-2026 neural architectures beyond transformers.

proposals.title: Deterministic Sparse Training via Blueprint-Guided FPGA Acceleration: Bridging SLIDE's Efficiency with Hardware Predictability
proposals.motivation: Training large neural networks is computationally prohibitive. Sparse training algorithms like SLIDE (based on Locality-Sensitive Hashing) offer significant FLOP reduction but introduce dynamic, data-dependent randomness, making them inefficient on standard accelerators like GPUs and ill-suited for deterministic hardware like FPGAs. This creates a critical gap where algorithmic sparsity cannot translate to practical hardware efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by decoupling the generation of sparsity patterns from the core training computation, we can transform SLIDE's dynamic randomness into a static, pre-computed 'blueprint' schedule, enabling the design of a deterministic, high-throughput FPGA accelerator for sparse training.
proposals.method: We propose a co-designed system: (1) A host CPU runs the SLIDE algorithm offline to generate deterministic, batched sparsity patterns (layer-wise activation/gradient masks) for multiple training steps, stored as 'blueprints'. (2) We design a fixed-pipeline FPGA accelerator with optimized memory hierarchies (leveraging HBM if available) and processing engines that consume these blueprints. The hardware performs only the scheduled sparse-dense matrix multiplications, achieving predictable, high-bandwidth memory access and parallel computation, inspired by the static scheduling principles in works like FlexGen but applied to training.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a fully functional, open-source algorithm-hardware co-design framework for sparse training, demonstrating superior energy efficiency and throughput compared to GPU-based SLIDE implementations. It will provide a novel methodology for bridging probabilistic algorithms with deterministic hardware, expanding the applicability of FPGAs in adaptive machine learning workloads.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the 'blueprinting' abstraction that explicitly separates stochastic pattern discovery from deterministic execution, a non-obvious shift from prior co-design efforts that typically accelerate fixed, static sparsity. This allows us to preserve SLIDE's algorithmic benefits while achieving hardware efficiency previously only possible for inference or dense operations.

proposals.title: COMM-SLIDE: Communication-Efficient Distributed Training via Hierarchical Hashing and Stale-Gradient Alignment
proposals.motivation: The SLIDE algorithm demonstrated that Locality-Sensitive Hashing (LSH) can accelerate large neural network training on CPUs by sparsely activating neurons, reducing FLOPs. However, scaling SLIDE to distributed CPU clusters is bottlenecked by the high communication cost of synchronizing the rapidly changing hash-driven sparse activation patterns across workers. Pre-2026 distributed training research, including Chen's work on FlexGen and InRank, focuses on managing memory and computation, but efficient communication for dynamic sparse workloads remains a critical open challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a two-tier hierarchical hashing scheme, coupled with a staleness-aware gradient aggregation protocol, can decouple local exploration of hash collisions from global weight stability, thereby reducing synchronization overhead and enabling near-linear scaling of SLIDE on distributed CPU clusters.
proposals.method: We will design a system where each worker maintains a local LSH table for per-iteration, fine-grained candidate neuron selection, while a global, slower-updating hash table tracks stable high-weight neurons. A gradient aggregation protocol will exploit the inherent sparsity of LSH-selected activations, allowing workers to proceed with local updates using slightly stale global weights, synchronizing only critical gradients. This builds upon Chen's expertise in efficient inference (FlexGen) and low-rank updates (InRank) to manage communication latency and model state.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first communication-optimized, distributed implementation of the SLIDE algorithm, enabling the practical use of large-scale CPU clusters for training massive neural networks. It will contribute a novel hierarchical hashing architecture and a formal analysis of staleness tolerance in hash-based sparse training, advancing the field of efficient distributed learning.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior distributed training frameworks that optimize dense communication, COMM-SLIDE explicitly targets the unique, dynamic sparsity pattern of LSH-based training. The co-design of hierarchical hashing for structural sparsity and a staleness-aware protocol for temporal sparsity is a novel approach to breaking the communication bottleneck.

proposals.title: Distributed SLIDE: Scaling Sub-Linear Deep Learning via Sparse, Hash-Based Synchronization Inspired by Radio Astronomy Correlation
proposals.motivation: The SLIDE algorithm enables efficient large-model training on CPUs via locality-sensitive hashing (LSH) and sparse attention, but its distributed scaling is bottlenecked by synchronizing massive, dynamic hash tables across nodes. Pre-2026, distributed training frameworks like FlexGen focus on memory and time trade-offs for inference, not the unique sparse synchronization challenge of hash-based training. Scaling SLIDE to multi-node CPU clusters is crucial for making large-scale training hardware-accessible.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting the distributed 'FX' correlation architecture from Very Long Baseline Interferometry (VLBI), where local frequency filtering precedes central correlation, we can design a distributed SLIDE system where sparse local updates are computed independently and merged centrally, thereby minimizing communication overhead and enabling scalable CPU cluster training.
proposals.method: We will design a distributed SLIDE architecture where each worker node performs LSH and sparse forward/backward passes on a local data partition, generating a compressed set of sparse weight updates (key-value pairs for affected hash buckets). Inspired by the FX pipeline, a central aggregator will correlate these sparse updates using a conflict-aware merging protocol. We will implement and test this using MPI on CPU clusters, evaluating protocols for synchronization frequency and merge strategies, extending principles from InRank's incremental low-rank updates to sparse hash structures.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first distributed training system for hash-based sub-linear deep learning, extending SLIDE's CPU efficiency to multi-node scales. It will provide a novel framework for sparse, communication-efficient synchronization applicable beyond SLIDE. The research will also contribute a documented analogy between distributed signal processing and machine learning synchronization patterns.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the cross-disciplinary adaptation of the radio astronomy FX correlation architecture to solve the hash synchronization problem in machine learning, a connection not explored in prior ML systems or distributed training work. The method specifically targets the sparse, irregular communication pattern of LSH-based training, unlike generic gradient averaging or parameter server models.

proposals.title: AdaHash-SLIDE: Adaptive Locality-Sensitive Hashing Selection for Efficient Neural Network Training
proposals.motivation: The SLIDE algorithm leverages Locality-Sensitive Hashing (LSH) to approximate attention and FFN layers, enabling training of large models on limited hardware. However, its performance is highly sensitive to the choice of LSH function, which is typically fixed and heuristic. As training progresses and the model's weight distribution evolves, a static hash function may become suboptimal, leading to degraded approximation quality and slower convergence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dynamically selecting or learning the most suitable LSH function during training, based on the evolving statistical properties of the model's activations and weights, will improve the approximation fidelity and convergence speed of SLIDE compared to using a fixed hash function.
proposals.method: We will develop a meta-controller that monitors key statistics (e.g., activation sparsity, weight matrix rank) during training. This controller will use a lightweight policy network, trained via reinforcement learning with a reward based on approximation error and throughput, to select from a candidate pool of LSH functions (e.g., SimHash, p-stable LSH) at regular intervals. We will integrate this adaptive selection mechanism into the SLIDE framework and evaluate it on standard transformer architectures for language modeling tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled method to optimize the core hashing component of SLIDE, making the training process more robust and efficient. It will contribute a new paradigm for adaptive, data-driven sparsification in large-scale model training, directly advancing the field of high-throughput, memory-efficient learning.
proposals.novelty: While prior work on SLIDE focuses on static hashing, our proposal introduces a learnable, context-aware selection mechanism for the hash function itself. This bridges the gap between fixed randomized hashing and adaptive model-aware sparsification within the LSH-based training paradigm.

proposals.title: Sub-Linear Learning for Data-Scarce Domains: Applying SLIDE to Few-Shot Classification
proposals.motivation: Many real-world applications, such as medical diagnosis or material science (e.g., predicting ceramic properties from limited experimental data), suffer from extremely small, high-dimensional training sets. Standard deep learning overfits, while classical methods lack capacity. The SLIDE (Sub-LInear Deep learning) algorithm, which uses locality-sensitive hashing (LSH) to approximate attention, was designed for efficient training on large datasets, but its properties in low-data regimes are unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the sparsity and inherent regularization induced by LSH-based attention in SLIDE can prevent overfitting and improve generalization in few-sample learning tasks, outperforming both dense transformers and classical ML models when training data is severely limited.
proposals.method: We will adapt the SLIDE architecture for few-shot classification by modifying its hashing-based attention mechanism to operate on very small token sets (e.g., <100 training samples). We will integrate principles from InRank for incremental low-rank adaptation to refine representations efficiently. Our evaluation will benchmark against standard few-shot baselines (e.g., prototypical networks, fine-tuned BERT) and analyze trade-offs in memory/time, drawing on insights from FlexGen and high-throughput inference work, across curated datasets from domains like computational materials science and medical imaging.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first rigorous framework for applying sub-linear deep learning to data-scarce problems, potentially unlocking efficient, high-capacity models for niche scientific domains. It will also provide new theoretical insights into how hashing-induced sparsity acts as a regularizer, contributing to the broader understanding of efficient attention mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: While SLIDE is known for large-scale efficiency, applying its core sub-linear computation principle to the opposite regime—tiny datasets—is a novel and counter-intuitive direction. The combination of LSH-based attention with incremental low-rank learning (InRank) for few-shot adaptation is unexplored.

proposals.title: Characterizing the Performance Regime Boundary: When Dense GPU Acceleration Outperforms SLIDE for Sparse Attention
proposals.motivation: The SLIDE algorithm demonstrated that hash-based sparse attention on CPUs could outperform dense GPU implementations for certain large models, challenging the dominance of hardware-accelerated dense linear algebra. However, the community lacks a systematic understanding of the precise conditions where this inversion occurs, leading to suboptimal system choices for training and inference. This work aims to provide a principled, data-driven characterization of this performance boundary.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the superiority of dense GPU methods over SLIDE is governed by a specific interaction between model architecture (e.g., attention head dimension, layer count), data sparsity patterns, and hardware memory hierarchy, and that this boundary can be modeled and predicted.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled empirical study using modified versions of FlexGen and InRank frameworks to profile performance across a synthetic and real-world task space. We will systematically vary key parameters: model scale (embedding dimension, number of heads), sequence length, batch size, and induced sparsity (via token importance scoring). We will measure end-to-end throughput and memory usage on a single GPU (dense PyTorch/TensorFlow) versus a multi-core CPU (SLIDE), analyzing the crossover point. Dense methods will be optimized using kernel fusion and activation checkpointing.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a practical decision framework for researchers and practitioners to select the most efficient computational backend for a given model and dataset. It will also identify architectural sweet spots for future accelerator design, informing hardware-software co-design for sparse-dense hybrid models.
proposals.novelty: While prior work established SLIDE's advantage in specific regimes, a systematic study of the inverse condition—defining when dense GPUs remain superior—is novel. The integration of incremental low-rank learning (InRank) concepts to modulate effective model rank during the analysis provides a unique lens on the density-sparsity spectrum.

proposals.title: Curriculum-Driven Monarch Induction: Stabilized Dense-to-Sparse Transformation for Efficient LLMs
proposals.motivation: Monarch matrices offer a promising, hardware-efficient structure for reducing the computational cost of large linear layers in transformers, but training models with this structure from scratch often leads to suboptimal performance compared to dense pre-training. Existing post-training pruning or factorization methods can induce instability and catastrophic forgetting, failing to preserve the rich representations learned during pre-training. A method to reliably convert high-performing dense models into efficient, structured sparse networks is crucial for practical deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: A curriculum-driven sparsification process, which gradually imposes the Monarch structure on a dense pre-trained model guided by parameter sensitivity, will yield a stable transformation that preserves model performance better than training a Monarch-structured model from scratch or applying aggressive one-shot pruning.
proposals.method: We will adapt and extend the incremental low-rank learning principles from InRank to structured sparsification. Starting from a dense pre-trained model (e.g., an LLM), we will iteratively apply a sensitivity-aware pruning mask that enforces the block-wise low-rank pattern of Monarch matrices. Sensitivity will be measured via a gradient-based importance metric. Each sparsification step will be followed by a short recovery fine-tuning phase, inspired by the recovery tuning in FlexGen, to stabilize the model. The final fixed Monarch architecture will undergo a comprehensive fine-tuning to close any remaining performance gap.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, stabilized pipeline for converting any dense pre-trained model into a hardware-efficient Monarch-structured network, maximizing the utility of existing model checkpoints. It will contribute a new benchmark for structured post-training sparsification, demonstrating superior performance over from-scratch Monarch training. The methodology will also offer insights into the stability of representations during constrained architectural evolution.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the *curricular application* of a fixed, hardware-efficient sparsity pattern (Monarch) to a dense model, controlled by sensitivity analysis to ensure stability, rather than discovering the pattern during training or applying it via one-shot pruning. This bridges the gap between the high performance of dense pre-training and the inference efficiency of structured matrices.

proposals.title: Dynamic Monarch: Learning Hardware-Efficient Structures via Activity-Dependent Pruning of Dense Networks
proposals.motivation: The pursuit of efficient inference for large language models has led to specialized architectures like Monarch matrices, which offer a favorable trade-off between expressivity and hardware utilization. However, such structures are typically hard-coded before training, potentially constraining the model's learning capacity and optimal structure discovery. This work challenges the sparse-to-dense paradigm by asking if superior, hardware-friendly factorizations can be dynamically *discovered* from a dense initialization, inspired by biological developmental pruning.
proposals.hypothesis: Starting with a standard dense weight matrix and applying a structured, gradient-guided pruning mechanism during training can dynamically induce a near-Monarch structure, yielding a model with final performance superior to one initialized with a fixed Monarch pattern and greater training stability than unstructured magnitude pruning.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Monarch matrix structure (a block-wise low-rank pattern) as a learned pruning mask. For each target layer, we initialize a dense weight matrix W and a trainable, continuous mask M of the same shape, initialized to all ones. During training, we apply a structured regularizer (e.g., a group L1 penalty on matrix blocks corresponding to the *complement* of a Monarch template) to drive M towards a Monarch-sparse pattern. The forward pass uses W ⊙ f(M), where f is a thresholding function. The gradient flow through both W and M guides which connections are preserved, mimicking activity-dependent synaptic pruning. Techniques from InRank for incremental low-rank learning may inform the mask schedule.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel training algorithm that merges the representational flexibility of dense training with the inference efficiency of structured sparsity. It will deliver a concrete method for 'growing' hardware-optimal structures, validated on benchmark tasks, and offer new insights into the interaction between training dynamics and final model efficiency, extending beyond pre-defined sparse training paradigms.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is inverting the standard design process: instead of baking in efficiency constraints, we let the training process discover them through structured pruning, explicitly drawing an analogy to developmental apoptosis. This contrasts with both static efficient architectures and post-training pruning methods that lack this guided, dynamic structural discovery.

proposals.title: Dense-to-Sparse Training via Dynamic Monarch Factorization
proposals.motivation: Efficient training and inference of large language models is constrained by memory and compute. Pre-2026, structured sparsity patterns like Monarch matrices are known for efficient inference, but they are typically baked into models from the start, potentially limiting the model's initial learning capacity and optimal structure discovery. Starting dense and inducing sparsity could preserve early learning fidelity while achieving final efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: That a dense Transformer model can be trained to high performance while dynamically and irreversibly factorizing its linear layers into Monarch structure, resulting in a final model that is both accurate and inference-efficient without the constraints of pre-defined sparse training.
proposals.method: We will adapt the incremental low-rank learning principles from InRank to structured sparsity. Starting with a standard dense Transformer, we will monitor the singular value spectra of weight matrices during training. Upon detecting stabilization, we will progressively replace targeted dense layers (e.g., in FFN blocks) with a Monarch factorization, freezing the Monarch 'butterfly' factors and continuing to train only the newly introduced parameters. This process will be guided by a token-level importance metric to prioritize layers for factorization, integrating concepts from FlexGen's memory-aware scheduling.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel training paradigm that decouples capacity from final architecture, potentially improving model quality for a given inference budget. It will provide a practical algorithm for dynamic, training-aware sparsification and contribute an analysis of the learning dynamics when transitioning from dense to structured sparse representations.
proposals.novelty: Inverting the standard sparse-to-dense paradigm by starting dense and inducing a hardware-efficient Monarch structure dynamically, rather than pruning a dense model or training from scratch with a fixed sparse pattern.

proposals.title: Monarch-Compress: Training-Aware Structured Compression via Distillation and Recovery Fine-Tuning
proposals.motivation: Deploying large language models (LLMs) is constrained by their massive memory and computational footprint. Post-hoc matrix factorization methods, like applying Monarch structures, offer promising inference speedups but suffer from significant accuracy drops due to approximation errors on pre-trained, dense weights. A training pipeline that bakes in structural efficiency from the start is needed to achieve high-throughput inference without sacrificing model quality.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating awareness of the Monarch matrix structure directly into the training pipeline via a teacher-student distillation framework, followed by a targeted recovery phase, will yield a compressed model that retains significantly higher accuracy than a model compressed via post-hoc approximation alone.
proposals.method: We will first train a conventional dense Transformer model as a high-accuracy teacher. A student model will be initialized with Monarch-structured layers via a structured projection (e.g., a least-squares factorization) of the teacher's weight matrices. This student will then be trained using a task-specific distillation loss (e.g., combining cross-entropy with KL divergence) from the teacher's outputs and intermediate representations. Finally, a brief recovery fine-tuning stage on the original task data will allow the student's representations to fully adapt to the structural constraints.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a concrete methodology for producing inference-efficient LLMs with Monarch-structured layers that maintain high task accuracy. It will provide empirical evidence on the accuracy-efficiency trade-off compared to post-hoc baselines and contribute a new pipeline integrating structured projection, distillation, and recovery fine-tuning for model compression.
proposals.novelty: While distillation and structured matrices are known separately, the novelty lies in the integrated pipeline: using a structured projection for weight initialization specifically tailored for Monarch matrices, followed by a dedicated recovery fine-tuning stage to mitigate the representational distortion introduced by the structural constraint from the outset.

proposals.title: MonarchCompress: Inference-Only Model Compression via Post-Training Monarch Approximation
proposals.motivation: Deploying large language models (LLMs) for high-throughput inference on resource-constrained devices remains a critical challenge. While training-time compression methods like LoRA exist, they require costly re-training or fine-tuning, which is prohibitive for many users with only a pre-trained model checkpoint. A method to achieve significant compression purely for inference, without modifying the original training pipeline, is urgently needed to democratize LLM deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Monarch matrix decomposition, a parameterization that generalizes block-diagonal and butterfly structures, can be applied in a post-training, inference-only manner to approximate dense weight matrices in LLM feed-forward and attention layers, achieving substantial compression and speedup with minimal accuracy loss.
proposals.method: We will develop a systematic framework to compress standard pre-trained LLMs (e.g., LLaMA, OPT) by replacing targeted dense linear layers with Monarch approximations. The method involves: 1) Layer-wise error analysis to select candidate matrices, 2) Applying a calibrated, iterative SVD-based algorithm to factor each chosen dense matrix into a product of Monarch-structured factors, minimizing the reconstruction error, and 3) Integrating the compressed layers into a system like FlexGen for end-to-evaluation of throughput, memory footprint, and accuracy on standard benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical, training-free compression tool for LLM deployment, directly reducing GPU memory pressure and increasing inference speed. It will provide a rigorous analysis of the trade-offs between Monarch rank, compression ratio, and task performance, establishing a new post-training paradigm for model compression.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior low-rank training methods like InRank, our approach requires no gradient updates or access to the original training data. It uniquely adapts the Monarch structure—previously explored primarily in training—as a post-training approximation tool, creating a direct bridge between algorithmic compression and inference system optimization.

proposals.title: Monarch in Practice: A Component-Aware Analysis of Optimization Landscapes in Modern Deep Learning Stacks
proposals.motivation: Monarch matrices offer a promising, hardware-efficient alternative to dense and fully low-rank layers for scaling large language models. However, prior theoretical analyses of their optimization landscapes are conducted in isolation, ignoring critical interactions with standard components like LayerNorm, GELU, and adaptive optimizers like AdamW. This gap prevents the principled deployment of Monarch matrices in real architectures, leading to suboptimal convergence and generalization.
proposals.hypothesis: The practical optimization trajectory and final generalization of Monarch-parameterized networks are governed not by the isolated Monarch landscape, but by its interaction with non-linear components and the optimizer, which can be characterized and exploited to derive superior training recipes.
proposals.method: We will first derive theoretical landscape properties (e.g., condition number, saddle point structure) for simple Monarch layers. Then, building on techniques from 'InRank' and 'FlexGen', we will design a controlled empirical framework to train Monarch-integrated transformer blocks. We will systematically vary components (e.g., LayerNorm placement, activation choice) and optimizer settings, measuring metrics like gradient variance, loss basin sharpness, and effective rank during training to map how the theoretical landscape is altered.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first component-aware theory for training Monarch-based models, yielding verified principles for initialization, learning rate schedules, and component ordering. It will directly enable more reliable and efficient high-throughput inference, as explored in Chen's prior work, by providing robust training foundations for these efficient parameterizations.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the hybrid theoretical-empirical methodology that explicitly models the interaction between structured parameterizations and the full training stack, moving beyond analyzing matrices in isolation—a non-obvious step given that most landscape analyses ignore these compounding, practical effects.

proposals.title: Monarch-Tensor: Hardware-Aware Co-Design of Tensor-Decomposed Monarch Matrices for Efficient LLM Training and Inference
proposals.motivation: The Monarch matrix structure has shown promise for efficient attention and MLP layers in LLMs, but its fixed block-diagonal pattern may not be optimal for hardware or parameter efficiency. Concurrently, tensor decomposition methods (TT, Tucker) offer powerful compression but are typically applied generically, ignoring hardware execution costs, which can lead to theoretical FLOPs reduction without actual speedup. A co-design approach is needed to bridge this gap.
proposals.hypothesis: By co-optimizing the tensor decomposition structure of Monarch matrix blocks with a hardware execution cost model, we can discover compressed representations that simultaneously maximize parameter efficiency, training stability, and inference throughput on target accelerators like GPUs.
proposals.method: We will develop a search algorithm that, for a given Monarch block, explores a space of tensor decomposition structures (e.g., TT, Tucker ranks). Each candidate is evaluated by a hardware-aware cost model, informed by kernel profiling data, that estimates actual runtime based on memory bandwidth, kernel launch overhead, and fusion potential. The optimal structure is then implemented as a custom, fused kernel that maximizes data locality, building on principles from systems like FlexGen but applied at the algorithmic compression level.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical framework for generating hardware-efficient, compressed LLM layers that achieve measurable speedups and memory reduction during both training and generative inference. It will provide new insights into the interaction between tensor decomposition choices and hardware execution, moving beyond FLOPs-based metrics.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that applies tensor decomposition in isolation or uses fixed efficient structures, this method jointly searches the algorithm (decomposition structure) and system (kernel implementation) design spaces guided by a direct hardware performance model.

proposals.title: Monarch-TT: Hierarchical Compression of Linear Operators via Nested Tensor Decompositions
proposals.motivation: Monarch matrices offer a promising structured, hardware-efficient alternative to dense layers and standard low-rank approximations for large-scale models, but their fixed block-diagonal structure limits ultimate compression ratios. Simultaneously, the field of quantum chemistry has successfully used Tensor Train (TT) decompositions to compress exponentially large state vectors into tractable simulations. Bridging these domains could unlock new frontiers in model compression.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying Tensor Train or Tucker decomposition internally to the blocks of a Monarch matrix will create a viable 'compression-within-compression' scheme, yielding extreme parameter reduction for large linear operators while preserving the computational benefits of the Monarch structure for efficient training and inference.
proposals.method: We will first formalize a Monarch matrix as a two-level hierarchical tensor. For each block within the Monarch structure, we will apply Tensor Train decomposition, parameterizing the block by a chain of smaller core tensors. We will develop specialized forward and backward pass routines that leverage this nested structure, integrating the approach into a PyTorch framework. The compression ratio will be controlled by the TT-ranks, and we will evaluate the method by compressing large feed-forward layers in Transformer models, benchmarking against standard Monarch and InRank baselines on tasks like language modeling.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel, highly compressed linear operator that merges hardware-aware structure (Monarch) with extreme parameter efficiency (TT). It will provide a new tool for high-throughput inference and training of massive models under severe memory constraints, directly advancing research on efficient attention and FFN layers. We will release open-source code for the Monarch-TT layer.
proposals.novelty: While tensor decompositions and structured matrices are both known, their hierarchical combination—applying TT inside Monarch blocks—is novel. This adapts a solution from quantum simulation to a core deep learning problem, creating a new class of compressed operators distinct from prior low-rank or block-diagonal approaches.

proposals.title: SparseGatedMonarch: Adapting Butterfly Matrices via Sparsely-Gated Learned Factors for Efficient Long-Context Learning
proposals.motivation: Monarch matrices provide a promising, theoretically-grounded parameterization for efficient long-sequence attention, but their fixed, algorithmically-defined butterfly factors may lack the task-specific adaptability of fully learned layers. This rigidity could limit their performance on complex, real-world data distributions compared to standard linear layers, despite their superior efficiency and generalization guarantees. Enabling controlled adaptation within the strict Monarch framework is a key challenge for making these architectures more performant while retaining their core benefits.
proposals.hypothesis: That a Monarch matrix can be made more expressive for specific tasks by allowing a small, sparsely-gated subset of its fixed butterfly factors to be replaced with learned counterparts, without sacrificing its foundational efficiency, invertibility, and spectral properties, leading to improved downstream performance on long-context modeling tasks.
proposals.method: We will design a SparseGatedMonarch layer that maintains the exact computational graph and parameter count of a standard Monarch matrix. A lightweight, input-dependent gating network will select a sparse set of butterfly factors to replace with learned, dense matrices. A novel regularization loss, computed on algorithmic data (e.g., Fourier, wavelet bases), will force these learned factors to approximate the functionality of the original fast transforms, preserving spectral bias. Training will use techniques from Chen's prior work on low-rank (InRank) and memory-efficient inference (FlexGen) to manage the added learning dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new hybrid parameterization that bridges fixed algorithmic transforms and learned linear layers, enhancing the adaptability of efficient long-context architectures. It will provide a practical method for injecting task-specific knowledge into structured matrices while maintaining their theoretical guarantees. The resulting layer is a direct drop-in replacement for standard Monarch matrices in transformers, aiming to improve perplexity and task accuracy without increasing inference cost or parameter budget.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its use of sparsely-gated, regularized adaptation within a fixed butterfly factorization, a constrained form of learning not explored in prior work on Monarch or butterfly matrices. The core innovation is the regularization strategy that ties learned factors back to algorithmic primitives, ensuring the modified matrix retains desired inductive biases.

proposals.title: SPARSE-FAST: Learning Fast Linear Transforms via Protein Folding-Inspired Structural Priors
proposals.motivation: Fast linear transforms, such as those parameterized by the Monarch structure, are crucial for efficient training and inference in large models, but their fixed, hand-designed sparsity patterns may limit representational capacity. Conversely, fully learned transforms offer flexibility but lose computational guarantees. There is a need for methods that learn transforms which are inherently fast by construction, merging the benefits of learning with algorithmic efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: By learning a sparse structural prior—analogous to a contact map in protein folding—that constrains the factorized interactions within a linear transform, we can generate Monarch-like matrices that are both highly expressive and guarantee sub-quadratic computational complexity.
proposals.method: We will develop a method where a lightweight network, conditioned on task data, outputs a sparse, binary adjacency matrix representing allowed multiplicative interactions between input and output dimensions. This 'interaction prior' will then parameterize a Monarch matrix, where non-zero blocks are aligned with the prior's edges and their values are learned. The training will jointly optimize the prior-generating network and the matrix values, using techniques from sparse training and low-rank learning (e.g., InRank) to maintain efficiency.
