Evaluation will focus on replacing dense layers in transformer blocks for tasks like language modeling, measuring accuracy vs. throughput.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new paradigm for learning hardware-efficient operators, directly translating a biological constraint-solving strategy into machine learning. It will provide a practical method to reduce the memory and time cost of model layers without sacrificing the benefits of learned parameterization, advancing high-throughput generative inference.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the conceptual transfer of the sparse contact map principle from computational biology to structured matrix learning, and the technical innovation of a learned, adaptive prior that dictates the factorization pattern of a fast transform, rather than using a fixed one.

proposals.title: SketchMonarch: On-the-Fly Data-Aware Compression for Adaptive Inference on Microcontrollers
proposals.motivation: Deploying adaptive neural networks on microcontrollers (MCUs) is hindered by the prohibitive memory and compute cost of storing or fine-tuning multiple model variants. Pre-2026, methods like Monarch matrices offer efficient, structured low-rank approximations for compression, but they are static and computed offline, lacking adaptability to dynamic on-device data. Enabling a single, ultra-compressed model to specialize its computations to live sensor context in real-time would unlock personalized, efficient AI on the most resource-constrained devices.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by fusing fast, streaming sketching algorithms with the Monarch matrix decomposition, we can dynamically estimate data-aware, low-rank factors for dense weight matrices directly on an MCU, enabling significant model adaptation without retraining or parameter updates.
proposals.method: We will develop SketchMonarch, a system that replaces selected dense layers in a pre-trained, compressed model. During inference, it uses Count-Sketch and Frequent-Directions algorithms to build a low-memory sketch of the batched input activations on the MCU. This sketch then informs a fast, iterative optimization (via power iteration or randomized SVD) to compute the near-optimal Monarch factors (block-diagonal with permutation structure) for the subsequent forward pass. We will integrate this with frameworks like TinyML and FlexGen, targeting ARM Cortex-M series MCUs, and evaluate trade-offs between sketch size, approximation quality, and latency.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel algorithm for data-aware, dynamic model compression requiring no backpropagation, 2) an open-source implementation for MCU deployment demonstrating adaptive inference within KB memory budgets, and 3) an empirical analysis of the accuracy-efficiency trade-off for on-the-fly specialization across sensor-based tasks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in using sketching not merely for acceleration, but as a core mechanism to enable structural plasticity (via dynamic Monarch factors) in a severely static deployment setting, allowing a single model to specialize to data context in real-time—a capability beyond static compression (InRank) or fixed efficient attention.

proposals.title: Adaptive Monarch: A Gated Multi-Fidelity Attention Architecture for Extreme-Scale Model Training
proposals.motivation: Scaling Transformer models to hundreds of billions of parameters is prohibitively expensive due to the quadratic complexity of self-attention. While structured linear approximations like Monarch matrices offer promising efficiency, applying them uniformly can degrade model quality by causing representational collapse in critical attention heads. An adaptive, learnable method is needed to balance computational cost with fidelity during large-scale training.
proposals.hypothesis: A trainable gating mechanism can dynamically route computations between standard attention and a hierarchy of Monarch approximations based on learned token- and head-specific importance, enabling reliable extreme-scale training without sacrificing performance on critical reasoning pathways.
proposals.method: We will design a novel attention layer where a lightweight, learnable router module assigns each token-head pair a fidelity score. Based on this score, computation is directed to either standard attention, a high-rank Monarch block, or a low-rank Monarch block. The router and the approximation blocks are co-trained end-to-end. We will integrate this with low-rank learning techniques (InRank) and memory-efficient systems (FlexGen) to establish empirical scaling laws for the trade-off between FLOPs, memory, and task performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, production-ready attention architecture for training models at the 100B+ parameter scale on limited hardware. It will provide the community with validated scaling laws for adaptive approximation and a framework for preventing representational collapse in efficient Transformers.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior static approximations, our method introduces a trainable, input-adaptive gating mechanism to create a multi-fidelity hierarchy within the attention operation itself, dynamically optimizing the compute-quality trade-off per token and head.

proposals.title: MonarchScale: Extreme-Scale Training and Inference of Large Language Models via Structured Low-Rank Monarch Approximations
proposals.motivation: Training and deploying models with hundreds of billions of parameters is prohibitively expensive in terms of memory and compute, limiting accessibility and innovation. The Monarch matrix structure, which factorizes dense layers into block-diagonal products, has shown promise for efficient approximation in smaller models, but its scalability and stability for extreme-scale models remain untested. This work addresses the critical need for fundamentally more efficient architectures to push beyond current scaling limits.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Monarch decomposition can be systematically applied and trained in multi-hundred-billion parameter models, maintaining competitive accuracy while drastically reducing the memory footprint and computational cost of both training and inference compared to standard dense or other low-rank approaches.
proposals.method: We will extend the Monarch decomposition to the feed-forward and attention projection layers of a decoder-only transformer, scaling to target sizes of 100B+ parameters. Building on Chen's expertise in InRank (incremental low-rank learning), we will develop a staged training protocol, initializing from a pre-trained dense checkpoint and incrementally adapting layers to their Monarch factorizations. For inference, we will integrate Monarch approximations with memory-efficient methods like FlexGen, optimizing token-wise throughput by exploiting the structure's inherent parallelism and reduced parameter count.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first comprehensive evaluation of Monarch matrices for extreme-scale LLMs, providing concrete metrics on the accuracy-efficiency trade-off. It will produce open-source tools for Monarch-based model compression and a scalable training recipe, enabling high-throughput inference of massive models on limited GPU resources.
proposals.novelty: While low-rank methods exist, applying the specific Monarch structure at this scale and developing a stable incremental training pathway from dense checkpoints is novel. The tight co-design of the approximation for both training stability and inference efficiency, informed by FlexGen principles, goes beyond prior isolated compression or inference optimizations.

proposals.title: Genome-Like Compression for On-Device Inference: Meta-Learning Monarch Matrix Genomes
proposals.motivation: Deploying capable models on memory-constrained edge devices remains a critical bottleneck. Pre-2026 work on efficient inference, like FlexGen, focuses on managing memory during computation, while low-rank methods like InRank compress parameters but still store explicit weights. A paradigm shift is needed to achieve extreme compression for tiny models, moving from storing parameters to storing a generative code for them.
proposals.hypothesis: The functional capacity of a neural network for a specific task can be encoded into a highly compressed, meta-learned 'genome' of Monarch matrix patterns, which a tiny, fixed on-device interpreter can execute, achieving superior compression-performance trade-offs compared to storing conventional low-rank or pruned weights.
proposals.method: We will design a meta-learning framework where the outer loop optimizes a compact genome—a set of instructions defining the structure and shared parameters of Monarch matrices within a base model architecture. The inner loop trains a small, fixed-parameter interpreter network (e.g., a minimal attention-based state machine) that reads this genome to dynamically reconstruct or apply the Monarch-based layers for inference. Training will use a bi-level optimization, leveraging techniques from InRank for incremental low-rank learning within the genome representation, and evaluate throughput and memory on a single GPU setup akin to Chen's prior high-throughput inference work.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel 'genomic' paradigm for model compression, decoupling the stored model code from the execution hardware. It will provide a method for extreme compression of task-specific models for micro-controllers and edge devices, and offer a new theoretical perspective on model representation inspired by biological data storage.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in framing Monarch matrices not just as computational primitives but as a meta-learned, generative encoding scheme, analogous to DNA. This moves beyond prior compression methods that store weights directly, instead learning to generate the model's function from a minimal code.

proposals.title: MonarchTiny: Extreme On-Device Compression via Monarch Matrix Factorization for Sub-10M Parameter Models
proposals.motivation: Deploying neural networks on resource-constrained edge devices requires models with minimal memory and compute footprints. Pre-2026, efficient inference methods like FlexGen and low-rank techniques (InRank) target large models, but extreme compression for tiny (<10M parameter) on-device models remains under-explored. Monarch matrices, which factor dense matrices into block-diagonal and permutation matrices, offer a promising but unvalidated path for radical compression in this ultra-small regime.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying Monarch matrix factorization to the dense layers of tiny transformer or MLP-based models will enable extreme compression (e.g., >90% parameter reduction) with minimal accuracy loss, outperforming standard low-rank and pruning methods for on-device inference.
proposals.method: We will adapt Monarch decomposition, pre-2026 used in large-scale settings, to replace dense weight matrices in sub-10M parameter architectures (e.g., TinyBERT, MobileViT). Building on Chen's expertise in low-rank learning (InRank) and high-throughput inference (FlexGen), we will develop a two-stage training pipeline: first, train a baseline tiny model, then apply a structured Monarch-aware fine-tuning algorithm that optimizes the factorized blocks under strict memory and latency constraints. We will benchmark against standard pruning and low-rank baselines on GLUE and a custom on-device task suite.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel compression framework specifically for ultra-small models, pushing the Pareto frontier of accuracy versus model size for on-device AI. It will provide the first systematic analysis of Monarch matrices in the sub-10M parameter regime, establishing new benchmarks for extreme compression.
proposals.novelty: While Monarch matrices are known, their application to and co-design with *tiny* on-device models is novel, moving beyond large-model compression. The integration of Monarch structure into an end-to-end, memory-aware training pipeline for this scale is unexplored.

proposals.title: Monarch Emergence: Data-Driven Induction of N-Dimensional Butterfly Factorizations from Dense Tensor Layers
proposals.motivation: Processing high-dimensional tensor data (e.g., video, volumetric scans) with dense linear layers is computationally prohibitive, while pre-defined efficient factorizations like Monarch may not align with the inherent structure of a given dataset. Current methods for model compression, such as pruning or low-rank learning (e.g., InRank), reduce parameters but do not actively induce a globally structured, hardware-efficient computation graph from scratch.
proposals.hypothesis: Starting from a dense, over-parameterized tensor transformation and applying a novel dynamic pruning and regularization strategy will cause the weight tensor to converge to an N-dimensional Monarch (butterfly) factorization, where the optimal axis permutations and separation points are discovered from the data's statistics rather than pre-specified.
proposals.method: We will design a dense tensor core layer with a high-dimensional weight matrix. During training, we will apply a combined objective: a task loss (e.g., reconstruction, classification) and a novel structural regularizer that promotes a Monarch factorization pattern. This regularizer will be coupled with an iterative, magnitude-based unstructured pruning schedule, progressively forcing the model to route information through a sparse, factorizable sub-network. A subsequent hardening phase will freeze the induced sparsity pattern into an explicit, fixed N-dimensional Monarch computation graph for efficient inference, drawing on principles from dynamic sparse training and Chen's prior work on high-throughput inference (FlexGen).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a new algorithm for inducing hardware-aware, structured factorizations from data, 2) a framework for generating task-optimal, efficient architectures for N-D tensor problems, and 3) empirical validation on domains like video understanding and 3D medical image analysis, demonstrating superior efficiency/accuracy trade-offs compared to static factorizations.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that applies a fixed Monarch structure or uses post-hoc pruning, our method discovers the factorization's geometry (axis permutation, partition points) dynamically during training, merging the flexibility of dense training with the inference benefits of a mathematically structured matrix.

proposals.title: InRank-Gate: Differentiable Input-Dependent Block Sparsity for Adaptive Inference
proposals.motivation: Dynamic sparsity methods like Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) and token pruning improve LLM efficiency but rely on fixed, heuristic schedules or routing, which are suboptimal across diverse inputs. Pre-2026 work on high-throughput inference (e.g., FlexGen) and low-rank learning (e.g., InRank) highlights the critical need for adaptive, fine-grained resource allocation that goes beyond static compression to minimize latency and memory without sacrificing accuracy.
proposals.hypothesis: A small, trainable controller network can learn to predict input-dependent, differentiable soft gates for candidate blocks within a model, enabling end-to-end discovery of superior dynamic sparsity patterns that outperform fixed schedules, and that these patterns can be consolidated into a permanently pruned and refined architecture.
proposals.method: We will attach a lightweight MLP controller to intermediate layers of a transformer, taking layer-normalized activations as input to predict gating scores for predefined blocks (e.g., attention heads, MLP sub-blocks). A Gumbel-Softmax relaxation enables differentiable training of the gates with a combined loss of task performance and a sparsity budget (L0 regularization). Periodically, we will prune blocks with low average gate scores and apply InRank-style incremental low-rank learning to duplicate and refine high-utility blocks, reallocating parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel paradigm for training input-adaptive sparse models, moving beyond static pruning or fixed MoE routing. It will provide a practical algorithm for dynamic inference that can be integrated with single-GPU high-throughput systems, and a methodology for evolving sparse architectures during training.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior dynamic sparsity or MoE methods, the gating policy is not heuristic but is a learned function of the actual activations, trained end-to-end with a differentiable objective. The subsequent consolidation phase permanently prunes useless blocks while amplifying useful ones, creating a final architecture specialized for dynamic, input-dependent execution.

proposals.title: MonarchSched: A Theoretical Framework for Convergence-Aware Dynamic Block Sparsity in Transformers
proposals.motivation: Monarch matrices offer a promising structured sparsity pattern for efficient attention and MLP layers, but their fixed, hand-designed sparsity structure may lead to suboptimal convergence and performance. Pre-2026 work on dynamic sparsity (e.g., gradual pruning) is largely empirical, lacking a theoretical link between sparsity schedules and the Monarch-specific optimization landscape. A formal analysis is needed to move beyond heuristics and derive schedules that provably improve training.
proposals.hypothesis: Dynamically adjusting the sparsity pattern of Monarch matrices according to a theoretically-derived schedule, guided by the geometry of the loss landscape, will accelerate convergence and yield better final models compared to static Monarch or empirically scheduled baselines.
proposals.method: We will first characterize the saddle point structure and Hessian spectrum of fixed-Monarch layers during training. Building on this, we will formulate the dynamic Monarch network as a bilevel optimization problem, where an outer loop controls block-wise sparsity via a learnable schedule parameterized by low-rank factors (inspired by InRank). The inner loop performs standard training. We will derive convergence guarantees for this process using tools from matrix perturbation theory and stochastic optimization, and validate the schedules on language modeling tasks using a FlexGen-like memory-aware training framework.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first theoretical framework linking dynamic block sparsity to convergence in structured efficient architectures. It will yield a practical algorithm, MonarchSched, for training high-performance sparse models with provable benefits. The analysis will also offer new insights into the role of sparsity structure in optimization, bridging the fields of efficient inference and low-rank learning.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in fusing the analysis of Monarch matrix optimization landscapes with principled dynamic sparsity scheduling, moving beyond static designs or empirical pruning. This theoretical grounding for adaptive block sparsity in a specific, popular structure is unexplored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: SparseSeed: Resource-Efficient Model Training via Dynamic Sparse-to-Dense Densification
proposals.motivation: Training large language models is prohibitively expensive in compute and memory. While sparse training methods like RigL and SNFS reduce FLOPs, they often sacrifice final accuracy compared to dense baselines. Conversely, dense fine-tuning is standard for adaptation but inherits the full parameter cost of a pre-trained dense model, limiting accessibility.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that beginning training from a sparse, randomly-initialized network and strategically densifying a learned, task-critical subset of parameters during fine-tuning will achieve competitive accuracy with dense models, while significantly reducing total training FLOPs and peak memory footprint.
proposals.method: We will first train a model from scratch using a dynamic sparse training algorithm, establishing a high-performance sparse base. For task adaptation, we will employ a learned importance criterion, inspired by InRank's incremental low-rank analysis, to identify a small subset of critical sparse parameters. These will be selectively densified (i.e., have their full parameter matrix restored) while the rest remain sparse. The densified subnetwork will then be fine-tuned, optimizing the memory-accuracy trade-off. Evaluation will use benchmarks from the FlexGen and related inference efficiency literature.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel training paradigm that decouples base model training cost from adaptation capacity. It will provide a practical algorithm for compute-limited researchers to develop capable models. We will also contribute analysis on which parameters are most valuable to densify for downstream task performance.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the standard 'dense-then-sparsify' pipeline to 'sparse-then-selectively-densify', a non-obvious approach for balancing efficiency and accuracy. It uniquely combines dynamic sparse training with a learned, parameter-wise densification strategy for fine-tuning, rather than applying uniform sparsity or low-rank updates.

proposals.title: DejaVu-Multi: Extending Contextual Sparsity for Efficient Inference in Encoder-Decoder and Multimodal Large Language Models
proposals.motivation: The DejaVu method demonstrated that contextual sparsity—predicting and reusing KV cache states across decoder-only LLM layers—can drastically reduce inference time and memory. However, the proliferation of powerful encoder-decoder (e.g., T5, Flan-T5) and multimodal (e.g., BLIP, Flamingo) models creates a critical gap. These models are computationally intensive for real-time applications, and their distinct architectures (cross-attention, modality fusion) present new challenges and opportunities for sparsity-based acceleration that remain unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the core principle of contextual sparsity, where KV cache states are predictable and reusable, generalizes beyond decoder-only transformers. Specifically, we posit that systematic sparsity patterns exist in the cross-attention mechanisms of encoder-decoder and multimodal LLMs, enabling significant inference speedups without accuracy loss.
proposals.method: We will adapt the DejaVu framework to target the cross-attention layers in encoder-decoder models (e.g., T5) and the vision-language fusion modules in multimodal models (e.g., BLIP). Building on Chen's expertise in low-rank learning (InRank) and memory-efficient systems (FlexGen), we will develop lightweight, incremental predictors to forecast KV cache similarity scores across these layers. The training will leverage token-level statistics and layer-wise correlations to identify reusable states, dynamically bypassing redundant computations during inference. Evaluation will measure throughput, latency, and memory footprint against dense baselines on standard text-to-text and vision-language benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first systematic application of contextual sparsity to encoder-decoder and multimodal LLMs, providing open-source implementations and benchmarks. It will expand the efficiency toolkit for a broader class of generative models, enabling their deployment in resource-constrained settings. The findings will also offer new insights into the computational redundancy and attention dynamics of non-decoder-only architectures.
proposals.novelty: While DejaVu focused on autoregressive decoder layers, applying contextual sparsity to cross-attention and multimodal fusion is novel and non-trivial, as the flow of information and sparsity patterns differ fundamentally. This extends a promising acceleration paradigm to the model families powering most conditional generation and multimodal reasoning tasks.

proposals.title: InRank-Predict: Efficient Incremental Predictors for Sparse Head and Neuron Identification
proposals.motivation: Identifying sparse, unimportant attention heads and neurons is crucial for model compression and efficient inference, as shown by work on model pruning and the FlexGen framework. However, existing predictor models for this task are often expensive to train and evaluate, creating a bottleneck for dynamic, large-scale model analysis. A low-cost, accurate predictor would enable real-time sparsity profiling and adaptive inference strategies for large language models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an incremental low-rank learning framework, applied to lightweight predictor architectures, can efficiently and accurately learn to identify sparse components by continuously adapting to new model states with minimal computational overhead.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage method. First, we design a lightweight predictor model (e.g., a small MLP or a factorized network) that takes as input a small set of layer-wise activation statistics and weight norms. Second, we train this predictor using the InRank (Incremental Low-Rank Learning) algorithm, where the predictor's weight updates are constrained to a low-rank manifold. This allows the predictor to be updated efficiently as new data (e.g., from different model checkpoints or input tokens) arrives, without catastrophic forgetting and with reduced memory/time costs compared to full fine-tuning.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical tool for rapid sparsity analysis in LLMs, directly reducing the cost of profiling models for compression. It will also advance the application of incremental learning techniques to meta-modeling tasks, demonstrating how low-rank adaptation can be used beyond primary model training.
proposals.novelty: While low-rank methods like LoRA are used for fine-tuning primary models, applying incremental low-rank learning (InRank) to train efficient *predictor* models for sparsity detection is a novel synergy. This approach specifically targets the efficiency of the predictor's own training loop, not just the target model's inference.

proposals.title: SPARQ: Synergistic Integration of Contextual Sparsity, Quantization, and Speculative Decoding for High-Throughput LLM Inference
proposals.motivation: Deploying large language models (LLMs) for real-time applications is bottlenecked by their massive computational and memory demands during inference. While individual techniques like quantization, speculative decoding, and sparsity optimization have shown promise, they are typically studied and applied in isolation, leaving potential synergistic speedups unexplored. Combining these orthogonal approaches is critical to achieving the next leap in inference efficiency, especially for resource-constrained environments like single-GPU setups.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that contextual sparsity (dynamically pruning attention heads and MLP neurons based on input) can be synergistically combined with low-bit quantization and speculative decoding to achieve multiplicative reductions in latency and memory footprint, without compromising model quality.
proposals.method: We will build upon the FlexGen framework and InRank principles to develop a unified inference system. First, we will adapt InRank's incremental low-rank learning to identify and prune contextually irrelevant model components (layers, attention heads). Second, we will apply post-training quantization (e.g., to INT4/INT8) specifically to the remaining active, high-rank pathways. Finally, we will integrate a lightweight draft model for speculative decoding, where the draft model's execution is accelerated using the same sparsity and quantization maps derived from the target model's context, ensuring consistency and maximizing throughput.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, co-designed inference stack that demonstrates greater-than-additive speedups from combining sparsity, quantization, and speculation. We will provide a rigorous analysis of the interactions between these techniques and release an open-source framework for high-throughput LLM inference on a single GPU, validated on benchmarks like code generation and long-form question answering.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the co-design and joint optimization of three major efficiency paradigms, moving beyond their isolated application. Specifically, using contextual sparsity patterns to guide both quantization granularity and speculative draft model execution is a previously unexplored integration.

proposals.title: SparseTrain: Leveraging Dynamic Contextual Sparsity for Efficient Large Language Model Pre-training
proposals.motivation: Training large language models (LLMs) is prohibitively expensive, dominated by the quadratic cost of attention over long sequences. While inference-time methods like FlexGen exploit token sparsity, training remains a full-computation bottleneck. Reducing the FLOPs of the forward and backward passes during pre-training would dramatically lower the cost and energy of developing capable models, a critical pre-2026 challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: The contextual sparsity patterns observed in trained attention heads—where a token primarily attends to a small, semantically relevant subset of other tokens—can be predicted and enforced during training via low-rank and sparse masking techniques, significantly reducing computation without harming final model quality.
proposals.method: We will integrate the InRank framework for incremental low-rank learning to identify and adaptively compress less salient attention interactions. Building on insights from high-throughput inference, we will design a dynamic, training-aware sparsity scheduler. This scheduler will use a lightweight auxiliary network to predict contextual sparsity masks for each layer and training batch, progressively increasing sparsity as training stabilizes, and ensuring gradient flow through masked positions is handled correctly for stable optimization.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel training algorithm that reduces the computational footprint of LLM pre-training. We will provide empirical evidence on standard benchmarks (e.g., C4, The Pile) showing reduced FLOPs and wall-clock time with minimal loss in downstream task performance. The method will bridge the gap between inference-time and training-time efficiency research.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work focused on inference or static sparsity, this proposal dynamically learns and applies contextual sparsity *during* training. It uniquely combines low-rank adaptation (InRank) with a trainable sparsity predictor, creating a co-design of model architecture and training process for efficiency.

proposals.title: HANS: Hardware-Aware Neural Architecture Search for Co-Designing Sparse Predictors and Execution Kernels
proposals.motivation: Sparse neural networks promise efficient inference, but their performance is bottlenecked by the mismatch between algorithmic sparsity patterns and hardware execution efficiency. Pre-2026, designing sparse models and their execution kernels is a sequential, sub-optimal process. A hardware-aware co-design is needed to unlock the full potential of sparsity for real-world deployment on diverse hardware, from single GPUs to edge devices.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a joint Neural Architecture Search (NAS) framework, which simultaneously optimizes a model's sparsity-inducing architecture (the predictor) and the corresponding sparse execution kernel parameters, will yield Pareto-superior solutions in the accuracy-latency trade-off space for a target hardware platform compared to decoupled design.
proposals.method: We will develop a differentiable NAS framework with a search space encompassing both the predictor's layer structure/attention patterns and key kernel parameters like tile size and scheduling. The search will be guided by a novel latency loss term derived from a lightweight, learned hardware performance model, trained on kernel profiling data (inspired by FlexGen's memory-time modeling). We will integrate incremental low-rank learning techniques (InRank) to efficiently evaluate candidate architectures during the search, and validate on vision and language tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel NAS formulation for hardware-aware sparsity co-design, 2) a set of Pareto-optimal sparse model-kernel pairs for common hardware targets, and 3) an open-source framework to democratize efficient model deployment, advancing beyond static compression methods like pruning.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight co-design loop within a single NAS optimization, moving beyond using NAS only for the model or hand-tuning kernels separately, and explicitly bridging the sparsity pattern search with low-level kernel implementation parameters.

proposals.title: Dual-Sparse Training: Creating Permanently Pruned Models via Predictable Activation and Weight Sparsity
proposals.motivation: The deployment of large language models (LLMs) is severely constrained by their massive memory footprint and computational cost, especially for high-throughput inference on resource-limited hardware. While dynamic sparsity in attention and activations is known, it is typically exploited only at inference time, failing to create permanently smaller models. A method to translate predictable runtime sparsity into a permanently compressed architecture is needed to reduce both storage and compute requirements fundamentally.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the sparsity patterns observed in model activations (e.g., in attention heads and FFN layers) during a representative calibration phase are sufficiently stable and predictable to guide the offline, irreversible pruning of model weights, resulting in a smaller, dense model that retains accuracy.
proposals.method: We will first profile a pre-trained LLM (e.g., LLaMA) using a diverse calibration dataset to identify consistently low-salience attention heads and neurons with persistently low activation magnitudes. Building on Chen's expertise in low-rank methods (InRank) and high-throughput inference (FlexGen), we will develop a dual-sparsity criterion combining this activation sparsity with weight sensitivity analysis. We will then permanently prune the identified structures and employ a short cycle of incremental low-rank learning (InRank) for recovery, fine-tuning only the remaining parameters to regain performance, resulting in a final, dense, and smaller model.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel model compression pipeline that produces permanently smaller, dense models ready for efficient deployment, directly reducing memory pressure. It will provide new insights into the relationship between dynamic activation sparsity and static weight importance. The resulting models will be directly compatible with existing inference systems, offering immediate improvements in throughput and accessibility.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that uses sparsity as a transient, inference-time optimization, our method leverages predictable sparsity to architecturally alter the model, creating a new, smaller dense network. It uniquely combines activation profiling with incremental low-rank recovery, moving beyond mere pruning masks to a learned, compact representation.

proposals.title: H2O-M: Hierarchical Heavy Hitter Attention for Million-Token Context Windows
proposals.motivation: As large language models (LLMs) are applied to long-context tasks like code repositories, scientific literature, and extended dialogues, scaling context windows to millions of tokens is a critical frontier. However, existing sparse attention methods like H2O (Heavy-Hitter Oracle) face significant memory and computational bottlenecks when naively scaled to such lengths, limiting their practical utility for truly massive contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating H2O's heavy-hitter identification with a hierarchical, token-grouping strategy and incremental low-rank updates, we can achieve sub-linear memory growth and maintain high-quality attention for context windows exceeding one million tokens on a single GPU.
proposals.method: We will adapt the H2O algorithm by first applying a multi-resolution clustering (e.g., using streaming k-means) to group tokens into hierarchical blocks, applying H2O's attention mechanism within and across block summaries. We will integrate InRank's incremental low-rank learning principles to dynamically update the representations of these summaries, minimizing memory overhead. The system will be co-designed with a FlexGen-like offloading and scheduling strategy to manage the massive KV cache, prioritizing heavy-hitter retention and efficient block swapping between GPU and CPU memory.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, production-ready attention algorithm capable of million-token contexts on commodity hardware, validated on long-document QA and code completion benchmarks. It will provide a principled framework for hierarchical sparse attention and demonstrate how low-rank incremental learning can be fused with caching strategies for extreme-scale inference.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic combination of H2O's statistical heavy-hitter approach with a dynamic, hierarchical token grouping and incremental low-rank updates, a method not explored in prior sparse or memory-efficient attention literature for this scale.

ideas.title: Combine sparse training with dense fine-tuning
ideas.description: Invert the paradigm by starting sparse and selectively densifying for accuracy.
ideas.sourceTitle: Pixelated Butterfly: Simple and Efficient Sparse training for Neural Network Models

ideas.title: Leverage hardware-aware neural architecture search
ideas.description: Use NAS to co-design the sparsity predictor and the sparse execution kernel for specific hardware.

ideas.title: Applying Locality-Sensitive Hashing to Convolutional Filters and Recurrent States
ideas.description: The original idea proposes extending the SLIDE algorithm's principles (using Locality-Sensitive Hashing to sparsify computations) to convolutional and recurrent neural networks. Drawing an analogy from computational genomics, where k-mer hashing pre-filters sequence comparisons in tools like BLAST, we adapt this solution to the deep learning context. The adapted research idea applies locality-sensitive hashing not just to fully connected weight vectors, but to the structural patterns inherent in CNNs and RNNs. For convolutional networks, we hash local activation patterns from filters to identify and compute only the most relevant convolutions for a given input region. For recurrent networks, we hash the temporal evolution patterns of hidden states to perform selective, approximate updates. This extends SLIDE's core efficiency mechanism from weight-space sparsity to activation- and state-space sparsity in more complex architectures.

ideas.title: Combine with other inference optimization techniques

ideas.title: Algorithm-Hardware Co-Design for Deterministic Sparse Training on FPGAs
ideas.description: This research introduces a co-designed system for sparse training that bridges the gap between SLIDE's algorithmic randomness and FPGA hardware efficiency. The key innovation is a decoupled execution model where a host CPU pre-computes deterministic, batched sparsity patterns (blueprints) offline, transforming dynamic randomness into static scheduling data. The FPGA is configured as a fixed-pipeline accelerator that consumes these blueprints to perform highly parallel, predictable sparse-dense matrix multiplications with optimal memory access patterns. This approach preserves SLIDE's computational savings while achieving the high throughput and energy efficiency of specialized hardware, overcoming the fundamental mismatch of a naive port.

ideas.title: Dual: Analyze training dynamics for previous token

ideas.title: Adapting Distributed Radio Telescope Correlation for SLIDE's Hash Table Synchronization
ideas.description: This idea proposes scaling the SLIDE (Sub-LInear Deep learning Engine) algorithm across multi-node CPU clusters by drawing an analogy from distributed radio telescope correlation in VLBI. The analogous 'FX' architecture, which performs local frequency filtering at each telescope before central correlation, inspires a solution for SLIDE's core challenge of synchronizing sparse, hash-based activations across nodes. The adapted research idea is to design a distributed SLIDE architecture where each worker node executes the locality-sensitive hashing and sparse forward/backward passes on its data partition locally, generating a small set of sparse weight updates. These sparse updates, rather than dense gradients or full model parameters, are then communicated to a central parameter server or aggregator for efficient merging. This approach aims to preserve SLIDE's computational efficiency on CPUs while minimizing the network bottleneck, enabling scalable distributed training without specialized hardware.

ideas.title: Monarch Matrices as DNA Compression for Tiny On-Device Models
ideas.description: This idea applies analogical reasoning from genomics and DNA data storage to the problem of compressing tiny on-device models using Monarch matrices. In DNA storage, massive data compression and robustness are achieved by encoding information into the structured, four-base 'alphabet' of DNA, using error-correcting codes inspired by biological systems. We adapt this solution by treating Monarch matrices not merely as efficient computational blocks but as a learned 'genetic encoding' scheme for the entire micro-model. The core research idea is to meta-learn an optimal Monarch-based 'genome'—a highly compressed, structured representation of the network's function—for a specific task. A tiny, fixed-architecture 'interpreter' on the device (analogous to a ribosome) would then execute this genome to perform inference. This approach aims for extreme compression by storing only the high-level structural code of the Monarch patterns rather than the full parameter tensor, directly addressing the scaling challenge of fitting capable models into severely constrained on-device memory.

ideas.title: Component-Aware Theoretical-Empirical Analysis of Monarch Optimization in Modern Deep Learning Stacks
ideas.description: This research formally analyzes the optimization landscape of networks using Monarch matrices, but crucially extends the theory to account for their interaction with standard deep learning components like LayerNorm, GELU activations, and AdamW. The core methodology develops a hybrid theoretical-empirical framework that first derives landscape properties (e.g., condition number, saddle point structure) for Monarch parameterizations, then systematically tests how these properties manifest and are altered during practical training with full network stacks. The outcome is a set of verified, actionable principles for initializing and training Monarch-based architectures to guarantee improved convergence and generalization compared to naive theoretical application.

ideas.title: Scale to mixture-of-experts model training
ideas.description: Adapt the scheduler for decentralized training of sparse mixture-of-experts models.
ideas.sourceTitle: Decentralized Training of Foundation Models in Heterogeneous Environments

ideas.title: Sketch-to-Monarch: On-the-Fly, Data-Aware Compression for Microcontrollers
ideas.description: This research fuses sketching algorithms with Monarch matrices to create a system for tiny on-device models that can perform lightweight, data-aware compression in real-time. Instead of pre-computing a static Monarch approximation offline, the method uses fast randomized sketches of live sensor or input data on the microcontroller to dynamically estimate the optimal, low-rank Monarch factors for a dense weight matrix. This allows a single, ultra-compressed model to specialize its key computational blocks to the current context or user without retraining, achieving adaptive performance within strict memory and compute budgets. The synergy lies in using sketching not just for acceleration, but as an enabler for on-device, data-driven structural plasticity in severely resource-constrained environments.

ideas.title: Invert the sparse-to-dense training paradigm

ideas.sourceTitle: Monarch: Expressive Structured Matrices for Efficient and Accurate Training

ideas.title: Adaptive Multi-Fidelity Monarch for Reliable Extreme-Scale Training
ideas.description: This research develops and validates an adaptive Monarch architecture designed to scale reliably to models with hundreds of billions of parameters. Instead of a uniform approximation, the system integrates a trainable gating router that dynamically allocates compute between standard attention and a hierarchy of Monarch approximations based on learned importance. This ensures critical attention mechanisms retain high fidelity while less sensitive operations use efficient approximations, preventing representational collapse. The project will establish scaling laws for the adaptive framework to predict the computational trade-offs and quality guarantees before committing to exascale training runs.

ideas.title: Scale H2O to massive context windows

ideas.title: Invert homogeneous data parallelism focus

ideas.title: Curriculum-Driven Structured Sparsification: Dense-to-Monarch Induction with Stability Guarantees
ideas.description: This approach systematically converts a pre-trained dense model into an efficient Monarch-structured network via a stabilized, curriculum-based process. It begins with a fully dense pre-training phase to establish robust feature representations. A sensitivity-aware pruning algorithm then gradually imposes the Monarch matrix factorization pattern, guided by parameter importance metrics to avoid catastrophic forgetting. Each sparsification step is followed by a short recovery fine-tuning phase, culminating in a final tuning of the now-fixed sparse architecture to ensure performance matches or exceeds that of a model trained with Monarch structure from inception.

ideas.title: COMM-SLIDE: Communication-Aware Distributed Training via Hierarchical Hashing and Stale-Gradient Alignment
ideas.description: COMM-SLIDE is a redesigned system for scaling the SLIDE algorithm to distributed CPU clusters that directly targets the communication bottleneck. It uses a hierarchical hashing structure to minimize synchronization overhead, maintaining a global hash table for stable weights and local tables for rapid, iteration-specific updates. The system incorporates a staleness-aware gradient aggregation protocol that exploits the sparsity of LSH-selected activations, allowing nodes to proceed with minimal blocking. This approach preserves SLIDE's computational efficiency while making distributed execution communication-viable, enabling near-linear scaling on commodity CPU clusters.

ideas.title: Apply Scatterbrain to more vision and language tasks
ideas.description: Test Scatterbrain as a drop-in replacement in other large-scale pre-trained vision and language models beyond T2T-ViT and BigGAN.
ideas.sourceTitle: Scatterbrain: Unifying Sparse and Low-rank Attention Approximation

ideas.title: Pruning a Dense Network via Developmental Apoptosis
ideas.description: This idea inverts the sparse-to-dense paradigm by starting with a dense model and dynamically inducing Monarch structure, challenging the assumption that efficient structures must be baked in from the start. The analogy is drawn from developmental biology, where organisms initially create an overabundance of neural connections and then prune them down to an optimal, efficient network through activity-dependent apoptosis. The adapted solution initializes a standard dense weight matrix. During training, a structured pruning mechanism, guided by a combination of weight magnitude and gradient flow, is applied to progressively zero out elements outside a learned Monarch pattern. This process dynamically carves the efficient, hardware-friendly Monarch structure out of the dense starting point, allowing the model to discover the optimal sparse factorization through training, potentially leading to better final performance and stability than pre-defined sparse initialization.

ideas.title: Dual: use sparsity for model compression

ideas.title: Hardware-Aware Co-Design of Tensor-Decomposed Monarch Blocks
ideas.description: This research introduces a hardware-aware co-design framework for compressing Monarch matrices via tensor decomposition. Instead of applying decomposition generically, the method uses a cost model of target accelerator execution (e.g., GPU memory bandwidth, kernel launch overhead) to guide the search for optimal decomposition structures (TT, Tucker) within each block-diagonal factor. The resulting compressed blocks are not only parameter-efficient but are also restructured into computation kernels that maximize data locality and enable fusion, ensuring the theoretical compression translates into practical speedups and reduced memory footprint during training and inference.

ideas.title: Dynamically Structured Monarch Networks: A Theoretical Framework for Adaptive Block Sparsity
ideas.description: This research direction fuses the dynamic adjustment of block sparsity with a formal analysis of the Monarch matrix optimization landscape. The core idea is to model the training of a Monarch-based neural network layer not as a static sparse structure, but as a dynamic process where blocks are pruned or grown according to a schedule. The theoretical analysis will characterize how this dynamic sparsity alters the loss landscape—specifically, how it can help escape saddle points or shallow minima inherent to the fixed Monarch structure. The outcome is a set of theoretically-grounded schedules for varying sparsity that are proven to improve convergence and final performance, moving beyond empirical heuristics for dynamic sparsity.

ideas.title: Analyze training with non-predictive losses

ideas.sourceTitle: Scan and Snap: Understanding Training Dynamics and Token Composition in 1-layer Transformer

ideas.title: Integrate learned fast transforms via protein folding-inspired sparse priors
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational biology—using sparse structural priors to efficiently predict complex 3D protein folds—to the problem of learning fast linear transforms like the Monarch parameterization. In the analogous field, sparse contact maps derived from evolutionary data constrain the vast folding search space to a tractable, physically realistic manifold. The adapted solution is to learn not a dense transform matrix, but a sparse prior (e.g., a learned set of core multiplicative constraints or a low-dimensional interaction graph) that encapsulates the essential structure for computational efficiency. This learned prior then directly informs or generates the factorized, hardware-efficient pattern of the Monarch matrix, integrating the representational power of learning with the algorithmic guarantees of fast transforms. The result is a method that learns transforms which are inherently fast, by construction, due to the biologically-inspired sparse structural guidance.

ideas.title: Monarch-Compress via Structured Distillation and Recovery Fine-Tuning
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the post-hoc approximation flaw by integrating Monarch structure awareness into the training pipeline. It first trains a conventional dense model to serve as a high-accuracy teacher. A student model with Monarch-structured layers is then initialized via a structured projection of the teacher's weights and trained using task-specific distillation loss. A final, brief recovery fine-tuning stage allows the student's representations to fully adapt to the structural constraints, yielding a compressed, inference-ready model with significantly better accuracy retention than a pure post-hoc approximation.

ideas.title: Apply Quantum Circuit Compression via Tensor Decomposition to Monarch Blocks
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from quantum computing/chemistry, where Tensor Train (Matrix Product State) decomposition compresses high-dimensional wavefunction tensors for simulation feasibility. The analogous problem is compressing the high-parameter-count blocks within a Monarch matrix's block-diagonal structure. The adapted solution is to apply Tensor Train or Tucker decomposition internally to each Monarch block, treating the overall matrix as a two-level hierarchical tensor. This creates a 'compression-within-compression' method, potentially yielding extreme parameter reduction for large-scale linear operators in deep learning models while maintaining efficient approximate computation.

ideas.title: Monarch with Sparsely-Gated Adaptive Butterfly Factors
ideas.description: This approach maintains the exact computational graph and parameter budget of a standard Monarch matrix for efficient long-sequence modeling, but allows a small, sparsely-gated subset of its fixed butterfly factors to be replaced by learned counterparts. The learning is governed by a regularization loss that forces the learned factors to approximate the functionality of the original fast transforms on algorithmic data, ensuring foundational spectral properties are retained. The system thus gains the adaptability of learned transforms where beneficial, while strictly preserving the efficiency, invertibility, and generalization guarantees of the original parameterization.

ideas.title: Differentiable Input-Dependent Policy for Dynamic Block Sparsity
ideas.description: This method replaces fixed schedules with a learned policy for dynamic sparsity. A small, trainable controller network observes intermediate activations and outputs soft gates for candidate blocks, allowing for input-dependent sparsity patterns. The gating mechanism is trained end-to-end with a relaxed, differentiable objective that balances task loss with a sparsity budget, enabling the model to discover which blocks are useful. Periodically, blocks with gate statistics below a threshold are permanently pruned, while resources are reallocated to duplicate and refine high-utility blocks, creating a fully adaptive architecture.

ideas.title: Dynamic, Data-Driven Induction of N-Dimensional Monarch Structure from Dense Models
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel paradigm for designing efficient architectures for high-dimensional tensor data (e.g., video, 3D medical imaging, scientific simulations). Instead of pre-defining a Monarch-like factorization for 3D/ND data or starting from a sparse structure, we begin with a fully dense, over-parameterized tensor transformation layer. During training, a dynamic, data-driven pruning and structural induction algorithm is applied to progressively reveal and harden an N-dimensional Monarch factorization within the dense weight tensor. This allows the model to discover the most effective axes for separation and the optimal permutation of modes for the butterfly pattern directly from the data's statistics, leading to a final model that is both computationally structured and maximally performant for its specific task.

ideas.title: Apply compressive sensing techniques from MRI to accelerate dense-to-Monarch approximation
ideas.description: This idea adapts compressive sensing techniques from medical imaging, specifically MRI, to accelerate the dense-to-Monarch approximation step. In MRI, compressive sensing recovers high-resolution images from randomly undersampled frequency data by leveraging transform-domain sparsity. Analogously, we treat the dense neural network weight matrix as a signal to be acquired. We use randomized linear algebra sketching (like a random projection matrix) to take a small number of linear measurements of the dense matrix. Instead of a standard least-squares fit to the sketch, we formulate and solve a reconstruction problem that directly enforces the target Monarch matrix structure (as a combined block-low-rank and butterfly constraint) as a prior, minimizing the measurement error under this structural constraint. This could yield a more accurate and stable Monarch approximation from fewer sketches compared to a simple linear algebraic fit.

ideas.title: Adaptive Neural Pruning via Ecological Succession Dynamics
ideas.description: This idea adapts the ecological concept of succession to dynamically vary block sparsity during neural network training. The analogous solution from ecology involves a landscape transitioning from an initial state with many fast-colonizing pioneer species to a mature state with a stable, efficient community of specialists. Adapted to machine learning, training begins with a dense population of many small, computationally inexpensive blocks (pioneers). As training progresses, a pruning criterion based on both performance contribution and temporal stability (analogous to ecological fitness and resilience) is applied, aggressively removing transient blocks while allowing robust, specialized blocks (climax species) to consolidate and potentially grow in capacity. The training regime itself is divided into distinct 'successional stages,' where hyperparameters like learning rate and pruning rate are adjusted to first encourage rapid exploration and then refinement towards an efficient, sparse, and high-performing final architecture.

ideas.title: Structure-Adaptive Compositional Factorization (SACF) for High-Dimensional Data
ideas.description: This method generalizes the Monarch principle by learning a data-adaptive factorization graph for N-dimensional tensors. A lightweight gating network analyzes input correlations to select a sequence of pairwise mode permutations and factorizations, constructing a hierarchy of efficient 2D operations. This approach maintains the invertibility and parameter efficiency of Monarch while explicitly modeling complex, non-grid-like dependencies found in video, volumetric, and multi-modal data. The result is a scalable architecture that avoids the exponential blow-up of a naive ND extension.

ideas.title: Inverse Sparse Coding for Monarch Matrices
ideas.description: This idea expands the inverse Monarch approximation problem via analogy to the inverse sparse coding problem in computational neuroscience. In sparse coding, a signal is reconstructed from its sparse representation using a known dictionary. Here, the Monarch matrix is interpreted as a structured, efficient 'code' for a dense matrix, following a known factorization pattern (e.g., a product of block-diagonal matrices). The analogous solution—reconstructing the original signal from its code via constrained optimization—is adapted. The research will develop methods to, given a Monarch matrix, find the dense matrix it best approximates by solving an inverse optimization problem that minimizes reconstruction error under constraints such as matrix norm, rank, or spectral properties, effectively solving for the 'signal' most likely to have generated this efficient approximation.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Monarch: Scaling via Astrophysical Structure Formation Analogy
ideas.description: This idea proposes to scale Monarch training and approximation to extreme model sizes (hundreds of billions of parameters) by drawing an analogy from astrophysical structure formation. The analogous problem is simulating gravitational interactions in a universe of N bodies, which is solved using hierarchical multi-grid methods like the Barnes-Hut algorithm, grouping distant particles to reduce O(N²) complexity to O(N log N). The adapted solution is 'Hierarchical Monarch,' a multi-resolution matrix factorization. Critical, dense parameter blocks (e.g., in attention heads or expert layers) are approximated with high-fidelity Monarch structures, while vast regions of sparser interactions are aggregated into highly compressed, low-rank Monarch super-blocks. This creates a variable-fidelity representation of the model's parameter matrix, acting as a computational telescope that focuses precision where it's most needed, thereby making the training and inference of extreme-scale Monarch-based models computationally tractable.

ideas.title: Use neural architecture search for Monarch structure

ideas.title: Optimization Framework for Loss-Aware Dense-to-Monarch Approximation
ideas.description: This research develops a constructive optimization system to generate dense-to-Monarch approximations that are explicitly optimal for specified loss metrics. Instead of merely analyzing an existing analytical solution, the framework treats the approximation's parameters (e.g., block sizes, permutation strategies) as optimizable variables subject to the Monarch structural constraint. It then employs tailored optimization algorithms—such as projected gradient descent or evolutionary strategies—to minimize diverse loss functions, including spectral, Frobenius, or task-specific losses. This approach guarantees a practically superior approximation by directly searching the constrained parameter space for the best possible solution under each metric.

ideas.title: Evolutionary NAS for Adaptive Monarch Matrix Factorization
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from computational drug discovery, where evolutionary algorithms efficiently search a combinatorial space of molecular structures, to the problem of finding optimal Monarch matrix structures. Instead of using pure reinforcement learning or gradient-based NAS, we propose an evolutionary neural architecture search (NAS) framework. A population of Monarch matrix designs, defined by their block sizes, permutation strategies, and potential hierarchical decompositions, is maintained. Each design is instantiated within a model and evaluated on a downstream task. The top-performing designs are selected to produce the next generation through operations like crossover (swapping blocks or permutation patterns between two good designs) and mutation (randomly altering a block size or sparsity). This bio-inspired, population-based approach allows for a more global and efficient exploration of the discrete, non-differentiable Monarch parameter space, automating the discovery of high-performance, hardware-efficient factorizations.

ideas.title: Optimality of Dense-to-Monarch Approximation Under Varied Loss: An Analogical Approach from Control Theory
ideas.description: This idea expands the original challenge to the optimality of the dense-to-Monarch analytical approximation by drawing an analogy from control theory. In that field, approximating a complex controller with a simpler, structured one is common, and the standard solution optimized for one norm (e.g., H-infinity) is often found suboptimal for other critical performance metrics. Adapting this solution, the research will investigate if the standard analytical approximation for Monarch matrices is optimal under a diverse set of loss metrics beyond the typical Frobenius norm. This involves defining and evaluating against metrics like spectral norm, training dynamics stability, and hardware efficiency, potentially leading to new, more task-optimal approximation methods for structured efficient transformers.

ideas.title: Jointly Optimizing Block Structure and Approximation Loss for Adaptive Monarch Matrices
ideas.description: This research direction fuses the structural flexibility of irregular Monarch blocks with a critical re-evaluation of approximation optimality. Instead of pre-defining a block-diagonal structure and applying a standard analytical approximation, we propose to co-optimize both the block layout (allowing overlaps, variable sizes, and irregular patterns) and the parameters within those blocks under a chosen loss metric (e.g., end-task performance, spectral norm error). This enables the discovery of Monarch-like factorizations that are not just more expressive due to their structure, but are also provably better approximations to a target dense matrix for a specific objective, challenging the universality of the standard analytical solution.

ideas.title: Monarch Optimization Landscape Analysis via Statistical Physics of Disordered Systems
ideas.description: This research adapts analytical tools from the statistical physics of disordered systems, specifically the study of spin glass energy landscapes, to formally characterize the optimization landscape of neural networks trained with Monarch matrices. The analogous solution from spin glass theory employs random matrix theory, replica methods, and TAP equations to map the complex, high-dimensional energy surface, quantifying properties like the distribution of metastable states and barriers. The adapted idea applies this framework to the Monarch matrix parameterization, analyzing how its specific block-sparse, low-rank structure shapes the loss landscape—examining the density and stability of critical points, the eigenvalue distribution of the Hessian, and the resulting implications for gradient-based optimization dynamics, generalization, and mode connectivity.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Composite Materials as a Blueprint for Hybrid Monarch Matrices
ideas.description: This research seed proposes using analogical reasoning from materials science, specifically the design of hierarchical composite materials, to investigate hybrid structured matrices. In composites, engineers synergistically combine materials with different properties (e.g., high-strength fibers and a resilient matrix) to create a new material with superior, emergent characteristics. The analogous solution involves modeling the effective macro-scale properties based on the micro-scale interaction of components. Adapted to numerical linear algebra, this means formally merging the Monarch structure (inherently good for hardware efficiency and certain linear transforms) with other patterns like low-rank (for parameter efficiency) or sparse (for conditional computation) into a unified, hierarchical matrix class. The research would focus on developing a theoretical framework to characterize the representational capacity, stability, and algorithmic efficiency of these 'matrix composites,' and empirically validate them in tasks like foundation model training or scientific computing, where different parts of a computational graph may benefit from different structural biases.

ideas.title: Error-Adaptive and Feedback-Controlled Sketching for Stable Monarch Approximation
ideas.description: This redesigned approach employs randomized linear algebra sketching not as a one-shot acceleration step, but within an adaptive, closed-loop control system. It continuously monitors the residual error and its effect on the training dynamics, allowing the sketch fidelity to be adjusted on-the-fly. This ensures the approximation introduces no more distortion than the optimization can tolerate, maintaining stability. The result is a robust acceleration method that guarantees convergence to a high-quality model comparable to the full-precision baseline.

ideas.title: Implement SLIDE on FPGAs using DNA sequence alignment hardware acceleration patterns
ideas.description: This research adapts hardware acceleration patterns from computational genomics to implement SLIDE on FPGAs. In bioinformatics, DNA sequence alignment algorithms like BLAST have been successfully accelerated on FPGAs using systolic arrays and specialized memory hierarchies that parallelize massive pattern matching operations. By adapting these architectural patterns, we can design FPGA implementations that efficiently parallelize SLIDE's locality-sensitive hashing operations, create custom memory controllers to stream weight vectors similar to DNA sequence streaming, and optimize the hardware pipeline specifically for SLIDE's computational pattern of hash-based candidate selection followed by sparse updates.

ideas.title: Apply Reverse Sparsification via a Generalized Architectural Taxonomy
ideas.description: This research seed proposes extending the reverse sparsification fine-tuning technique to other model families. Drawing an analogy from phylogenetics and comparative biology, the solution is to adapt the method of using a phylogenetic tree to map traits and predict their function in new species. The adapted idea is to first construct a generalized architectural taxonomy of neural networks, mapping the success of reverse sparsification onto specific, conserved structural modules (like attention mechanisms or feed-forward layers). This framework will then systematically predict and prioritize which untested architectures (e.g., state-space models, MoE models, or non-transformer backbones) are most promising for applying reverse sparsification, based on their structural homology to proven cases, thereby guiding efficient and principled future experimentation.

ideas.title: Overlapping Block Parameterization via Quasicrystal Tiling
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that the blocks in a Monarch-style parameterization must be strictly non-overlapping and regularly arranged. Drawing an analogy from condensed matter physics, specifically the modeling of quasicrystals, we propose a solution where matrix structure is defined by overlapping, irregular blocks. In quasicrystals, complex aperiodic order is described using overlapping tiles or atomic clusters (e.g., in Penrose tilings), often modeled via projection from higher dimensions or inflation rules. Adapted to this context, we would define a set of allowed overlapping block 'motifs.' The full parameter matrix could be generated by projecting a higher-dimensional, simpler block-diagonal matrix into the target space, or by applying a learned, iterative inflation rule to a seed block pattern. This allows parameters to be shared across traditional block boundaries, creating richer, more expressive structures while potentially preserving the computational advantages (like reduced parameters and structured sparsity) of the original Monarch approach.
